Thought complete The user is laying out a system where existing backend and
frontend packages with modular exports feed into an agent-driven workflow—when
someone requests a project like a beauty salon app similar to Fresha,
specialized agents analyze the requirements and select appropriate packages from
the catalog, then generate custom modules as needed based on blueprints. They're
now considering building their own code generator to handle this module
creation. For the CLI layer, they're looking at Laravel Prompts and Symfony
Console as the foundation—mirroring the patterns from the Laravel installer and
the nWidart Laravel Modules package, which has a rich set of stubs to work from.
I'm thinking through how to map this concretely: the CLI would orchestrate the
agent-catalog integration, guide users through the intake process, and trigger
the generation flow. The key is positioning this PHP CLI alongside the existing
Python generator with clear separation of concerns—the Python tool handles one
domain, the PHP CLI handles another, and they coordinate through the catalog and
agent layer. So they already have the packages built—what's missing is
machine-readable metadata about each one and agents smart enough to read that
catalog and pick the right packages based on user intent. When someone says "I
need push notifications," the backend agent knows to grab the notifications
package, the frontend agent grabs its React export, and they wire them together
automatically. For custom domain logic that doesn't exist in the catalog
yet—like beauty salon appointment scheduling with specific business rules—the
data modeler and solution architect create a blueprint, then the generator spits
out the module code following all the attribute-first patterns and dependency
injection rules. Now I'm thinking through how to structure the CLI itself—using
Laravel Prompts and Symfony Console to walk users through intake questions, then
orchestrate the agents to build out the project scaffold and wire everything
together. Yes — every piece of that vision lines up, and most of it maps to
infrastructure you've already built. Let me lay out the concrete architecture,
then walk the beauty salon scenario through it end-to-end.

What you already have as the catalog The packages ARE the catalog. Just needs
machine-readable metadata layered on top.

Backend packages — 18 tier folders under backend-packages/ (foundation,
framework, access, authorization, billing, compliance, finance, growth,
identity, notifications, observability, platform, products, sdk, shared, sports,
telemetry, workflow) — each already has a composer.json + service provider +
attribute-driven surface.

Frontend packages — 50 packages under packages/, each shipping core / react /
native subpath exports. This IS the tri-platform catalog — same package name,
three surfaces (@stackra/notifications, @stackra/notifications/react,
@stackra/notifications/native).

Domain-module blueprints — 28 under modules/** with a working Python generator +
validator.

Missing today: a queryable metadata layer that says "for capability X, use
package Y." That's the catalog.

The catalog schema One JSON per package, colocated with the package. No new
discovery mechanism — the package IS the source of truth.

backend-packages/notifications/notifications-push/ ├── composer.json ├── src/
├── catalog.json ← NEW: agent-consumable metadata └── ...

packages/notifications/ ├── package.json ├── src/{core,react,native}/ ├──
catalog.json ← NEW: tri-platform metadata └── ... Shape (catalog.json) —
attribute-first, consumed by every design agent:

jsonc

{ "$schema": "https://stackra.dev/schemas/catalog.v1.json", "name":
"@stackra/notifications", "tier": "frontend", "surfaces": ["core", "react",
"native"], "kind": "framework-plumbing", "purpose": "In-app + toast + push
notification delivery, with tenancy-scoped channels", "capabilities": [
"notify.info / notify.success / notify.error toasts", "in-app notification
center (bell + list + mark-read)", "push registration (Expo / FCM / APNs)",
"channel-based subscribe/unsubscribe" ], "when_to_use": "any feature that needs
to inform the user of async progress, background completions, or system state
changes", "when_not_to_use": "for realtime peer-to-peer messaging → use
@stackra/realtime; for cross-service events → use @stackra/events", "peer_deps":
["@stackra/contracts", "@stackra/http", "@stackra/support"], "backend_pair":
"stackra/notifications-push", "size_gzip_kb": { "core": 3.2, "react": 6.1,
"native": 5.4 }, "maturity": "stable", "owning_agent": "framework-core-builder",
"docs": ["packages/notifications/README.md"] } Every design agent's charter
grows one bullet — "Orient first: read catalog.json on every package the feature
might touch." Then solution-architect doesn't guess; it queries.

The CLI — Laravel Prompts + Symfony Console Yes, build it. PHP-native, matches
the runtime, reuses composer autoload, gives you Laravel Installer-caliber DX.

New package: backend-packages/foundation/cli (or a dedicated
stackra/installer under .ref/-style top-level tooling).

Structure mirrors .ref/installer-master:

backend-packages/foundation/cli/ ├── bin/stackra # global CLI entry ├──
composer.json # stackra/cli └── src/ ├── Application.php # Symfony Console
app ├── Commands/ │ ├── NewProjectCommand.php # `stackra new <name>` │ ├──
AddPackageCommand.php # `stackra package:add <name>` │ ├──
MakeModuleCommand.php # `stackra module:new <name>` │ ├──
GenerateModuleCommand.php # `stackra module:generate <name>` │ ├──
ScaffoldActionCommand.php # `stackra make:action <Name>` │ ├──
ScaffoldModelCommand.php # `stackra make:model <Name>` │ └── ... ├──
Concerns/ │ ├── UsesCatalog.php # reads catalog.json across all packages │ ├──
UsesStubs.php # stub renderer │ └── UsesBlueprint.php # blueprint reader ├──
Catalog/ │ ├── CatalogReader.php # aggregates every catalog.json │ └──
CatalogQuery.php # "find packages for capability X" └── Stubs/ # attribute-first
stubs (see next section) Each command uses Laravel Prompts for the interactive
path:

php

<?php

declare(strict_types=1);

namespace Stackra\Cli\Commands;

use Stackra\Cli\Catalog\CatalogQuery;
use Illuminate\Container\Attributes\Bind;
use Illuminate\Console\Attributes\{Signature, Description, Usage};
use Symfony\Component\Console\Attribute\AsCommand;
use Symfony\Component\Console\Command\Command;
use Symfony\Component\Console\Input\InputInterface;
use Symfony\Component\Console\Output\OutputInterface;

use function Laravel\Prompts\{text, select, multiselect, confirm, progress};

/**
 * `stackra new <name>` — scaffold a new Stackra project.
 *
 * Reads the master catalog, prompts for business shape, picks
 * default package set, clones the app template, then runs the
 * per-package installer for every selected capability.
 */
#[AsCommand(name: 'new', description: 'Scaffold a new Stackra project')]
final class NewProjectCommand extends Command
{
    public function __construct(
        private readonly CatalogQuery $catalog,
    ) {
        parent::__construct();
    }

    protected function execute(InputInterface $input, OutputInterface $output): int
    {
        $name = text(
            label: 'What should we call your project?',
            placeholder: 'beauty-salon',
            required: true,
        );

        $businessType = select(
            label: 'What kind of business is this?',
            options: [
                'academy' => 'Academy (sports / education)',
                'salon'   => 'Salon / spa / beauty',
                'gym'     => 'Gym / fitness',
                'clinic'  => 'Clinic / healthcare',
                'custom'  => 'Custom (I will describe it)',
            ],
        );

        $reference = text(
            label: 'Reference product? (e.g. fresha.com)',
            placeholder: 'fresha.com',
        );

        $capabilities = multiselect(
            label: 'Which capabilities do you need?',
            options: $this->catalog->allCapabilityGroups(),
            default: $this->catalog->defaultsForBusinessType($businessType),
        );

        // Resolve capabilities → packages via the catalog.
        $selection = $this->catalog->resolvePackages($capabilities);

        // ... clone template, install packages, run migrations
    }
}
The stubs — attribute-first, adapted from laravel-modules
The 60 stubs at .ref/laravel-modules-master/src/Commands/stubs/ are the starting shape, but they need to be rewritten to match Stackra conventions. The reference action.stub and model.stub I just read use the legacy shape (protected $fillable, handle() with no attributes) — we can't emit those.

Rewritten stubs/action.stub:

stub

<?php

declare(strict_types=1);

namespace {{ namespace }};

use Stackra\Routing\Attributes\{AsController, Middleware, Prefix, {{ verb }}};
use Stackra\Routing\BaseController;
use {{ dataNamespace }}\{{ requestData }};
use {{ dataNamespace }}\{{ responseData }};

/**
 * `{{ verb }} {{ route }}` — {{ description }}.
 *
 * {{ longDescription }}
 *
 * @category {{ module }}
 * @since    0.1.0
 */
#[AsController]
#[Prefix('{{ prefix }}')]
#[Middleware({{ middlewareArray }})]
final class {{ class }} extends BaseController
{
    public function __construct(
        {{ constructorParams }}
    ) {}

    /**
     * @param  {{ requestData }}  $data  Validated payload.
     * @return {{ responseData }}
     */
    #[{{ verb }}(
        uri: '{{ route }}',
        name: '{{ routeName }}',
        summary: '{{ summary }}',
        tags: ['{{ module }}'],
        requestSchema: '{{ requestData }}',
        responseSchema: {{ responseData }}::class,
        responseCode: {{ responseCode }},
    )]
    public function __invoke({{ requestData }} $data): {{ responseData }}
    {
        // TODO: implement
    }
}
Rewritten stubs/model.stub:

stub

<?php

declare(strict_types=1);

namespace {{ namespace }};

use {{ contractsNamespace }}\Data\{{ class }}Interface;
use {{ factoryNamespace }}\{{ class }}Factory;
use Stackra\Tenancy\Concerns\BelongsToTenant;
use Illuminate\Database\Eloquent\Attributes\{Fillable, Table, UseFactory{{ extraAttrs }}};
use Illuminate\Database\Eloquent\Factories\HasFactory;
use Illuminate\Database\Eloquent\Model;

/**
 * Eloquent model for {@see {{ class }}Interface}.
 *
 * @category {{ module }}
 * @since    0.1.0
 */
#[Table(
    name: {{ class }}Interface::TABLE,
    key: {{ class }}Interface::PRIMARY_KEY,
    keyType: {{ class }}Interface::KEY_TYPE,
)]
#[Fillable([{{ fillableList }}])]
#[UseFactory({{ class }}Factory::class){{ extraAttrsUsage }}]
final class {{ class }} extends Model implements {{ class }}Interface
{
    use BelongsToTenant;
    use HasFactory;

    /**
     * @var array<string, string>
     */
    protected $casts = [{{ castsList }}];
}
Every stub in the set gets the same treatment — actions use #[AsController]/#[Get]/#[Post]; models use #[Table]/#[Fillable]; repositories use #[AsRepository] + #[UseModel]; data DTOs use spatie/laravel-data attributes; commands use #[Signature] + #[Description]; jobs use #[Queue] + #[Timeout] + #[Tries]; interfaces are attribute-first with #[Bind]. About 60 stubs total, one long afternoon of authoring.

Where the Python generator fits alongside the PHP CLI
Don't retire it. Two different jobs:

Concern	Python (generate-module.py)	PHP CLI (new)
When it runs	CI + regen batches	Developer's terminal
Input	Blueprint JSON files	Interactive prompts + blueprint
Output	Full package tree	Full package tree + composer wire-up + migration run
Interactive	No	Yes (Laravel Prompts)
Runs migrations?	No	Yes (calls artisan migrate)
Registers in app?	No (blueprint edits require rerun)	Yes (updates app's composer.json + provider list)
Python stays for the machine pipeline — CI regen when a blueprint changes, batch regen across all modules. PHP CLI is the human pipeline — bootstrap a new project, add a package, scaffold a new module interactively.

Both consume the same blueprints. Both emit code that passes the same validator (validate-module-graph.py + Larastan level max + Pint).

The beauty salon scenario, end-to-end

User: "Create a beauty salon SaaS. Reference: fresha.com. I need:
       - Appointment booking with staff availability
       - Client profiles + treatment history
       - Push reminders 24h before appointment
       - Custom pricing per service
       - No sport-specific stuff"

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Phase 0: spec-intake-analyst                                    │
│   Reads user prompt + fresha.com competitive teardown           │
│   Emits:                                                        │
│     .kiro/product/intake/beauty-salon/                          │
│       ├── brief.md                                              │
│       ├── blueprint-draft.md   ← modules identified             │
│       ├── assumptions.md                                        │
│       └── reading-list.md                                       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                          ↓
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Phase 1-2: product-lead + stackra-product                    │
│   Emits: PRD + INVEST stories + salon business_type config      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                          ↓
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Phase 3: design-lead → 5 specialists in parallel                │
│                                                                 │
│   solution-architect queries CATALOG:                           │
│     "I need: booking + notifications + payments + profiles"     │
│     ↓                                                           │
│     catalog.resolvePackages(['booking', 'push', 'stripe']) →    │
│       Reuse existing:                                           │
│         stackra/foundation, tenancy, identity, auth, rbac,   │
│         subscription, entitlements, invitations,                │
│         notifications-push, storage                             │
│         @stackra/ui, auth, notifications, i18n, routing, ui,    │
│           theming, http, cache, forms, tables                   │
│       Custom modules needed:                                    │
│         appointments (booking + calendar)                       │
│         treatments (service catalog with pricing)               │
│         staff-availability (roster + timeslots)                 │
│                                                                 │
│   data-modeler writes 3 new blueprints under modules/salon/     │
│   api-contract-designer writes 3 OpenAPI files                  │
│   threat-modeler writes STRIDE for booking (PII + payments)     │
│   product-designer writes 8 screen contracts                    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                          ↓
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ CLI ENTRY (developer runs):                                     │
│                                                                 │
│   $ stackra new beauty-salon --preset=salon                  │
│                                                                 │
│   [Laravel Prompts]                                             │
│   ✔ Project name .................. beauty-salon                │
│   ✔ Business type ................. salon                       │
│   ✔ Selected packages (from Phase 3): 15 confirmed              │
│   ✔ Custom modules to generate .... appointments, treatments,   │
│                                     staff-availability          │
│   → Cloning apps/api-template → apps/beauty-salon-api           │
│   → Cloning apps/web-template → apps/beauty-salon-web           │
│   → Cloning apps/mobile-template → apps/beauty-salon-mobile     │
│   → Adding 15 catalog packages via composer.json + package.json │
│   → Running generate-module.py appointments (blueprint→code)    │
│   → Running generate-module.py treatments                       │
│   → Running generate-module.py staff-availability               │
│   → Running php artisan migrate                                 │
│   → Running pnpm install                                        │
│   ✔ Project ready at ./beauty-salon                             │
│   ✔ Run `cd beauty-salon && pnpm dev` to start                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                          ↓
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Phase 4: builders fill the 20% domain-specific gaps             │
│   laravel-feature-builder writes the booking-conflict logic     │
│   heroui-ui-builder builds the calendar picker composite        │
│   heroui-native-builder builds the mobile booking flow          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                          ↓
                    Phase 5-7 as normal
~70% of the code arrives from the catalog + generator. Agents write the interesting 20%. Templates provide the app-shell 10%.

What to build, in order
Order 1 — Catalog schema + populate (2-3 days):

.ref/schemas/catalog.v1.json — the JSON schema
One catalog.json per package (50 frontend + 18 backend tier folders = ~68 files, ~40 lines each)
CatalogReader service in backend-packages/foundation/cli/src/Catalog/
Update 6 charters (solution-architect, api-contract-designer, data-modeler, framework-core-builder, heroui-ui-builder, heroui-native-builder) — add "Orient first: read catalog.json" line
Order 2 — CLI skeleton (1-2 days):

stackra/cli package with bin/stackra binary
Symfony Console app + Laravel Prompts wired
Three commands stubbed: new, package:add, module:new
Global install path documented in root README
Order 3 — Three app templates (3-5 days):

templates/backend-app/ — Laravel 12 + Octane + Sanctum + tenancy + ONE example module
templates/web-app/ — Vite 8 + React 19 + HeroUI Pro + auth/tenancy providers + ONE example resource
templates/mobile-app/ — Expo SDK + HeroUI Native + Uniwind + ONE example screen
Order 4 — Attribute-first stubs (1-2 days):

Port ~60 stubs from .ref/laravel-modules-master/src/Commands/stubs/
Rewrite each to use current attribute conventions (see samples above)
Land under backend-packages/foundation/cli/src/Stubs/
Add a stub-renderer service that reads token replacements
Order 5 — Command implementations (5-7 days):

stackra new — orchestrate template clone + package installer + generator runner
stackra package:add <name> — read catalog, install via composer/pnpm, wire provider/module
stackra module:new <name> — interactive blueprint scaffold + validate + generate
stackra make:action <Name> / make:model <Name> / etc — single-file scaffolds using stubs
Order 6 — Extend the Python generator to consume the catalog (1-2 days):

Every generated module's composer.json lists its catalog.json-declared peer deps automatically
Regen still works — generate-module.py appointments --force respects catalog
Order 7 — Two new agents (1 day):

catalog-steward — owns catalog.json across all packages; ensures every capability change is reflected
cli-maintainer — owns the CLI + stubs + templates
Rough total: 2-3 weeks calendar time, ~15 working hours of author time (the rest is regen cycles, iteration on the DX).

Two things to lock in before starting
Where does the CLI package live? Options: (a) backend-packages/foundation/cli (bundles with backend monorepo), (b) .ref/installer-master-style top-level packages/cli (bundles with frontend monorepo), (c) separate stackra/installer repo (like laravel/installer). I'd go with (a) — it lives with the composer packages it installs, and Symfony/Laravel-Prompts want to be in the Composer autoload path anyway. The frontend Turborepo can invoke it via pnpm turbo run stackra:....

Do catalog.json files ship in the published package? Yes — they're the wire format between the catalog and consumers of the package outside the monorepo (SaaS consumers, third parties, future spinoffs). Include in composer.json extra.stackra.catalog pointer or a top-level catalog key in the JSON.

Say the word and I'll start with Order 1 (catalog schema + populate every existing package). That's the highest-ROI first step — every subsequent piece (CLI, generator upgrade, agent updates) plugs into it. Or if you want the CLI skeleton first so we have the invocation shell in place, I can do that first. Your call on sequence.

Est. Credits Used: 13.64
Elapsed time: 2m 17s

Restore
laravle prompts or https://github.com/pdphilip/omniterm better?

also the .stub file we will handle frontend with it or what? to create jsx and react files and for native also and so on ...?

Kiro

Thought complete
The user is asking me to compare Laravel Prompts with pdphilip/omniterm for CLI interactions, and also wants to know whether .stub files can handle frontend frameworks like React or if there's a different approach needed. I should look into omniterm to give a proper comparison, though I'm starting to check what that package actually offers. For the second question about stubs, the key insight is that .stub files are just text templates with token replacement — they're not PHP-specific at all. So they work equally well for generating React components, React Native screens, or any other text-based file format. The same token-replacement mechanism handles everything. I'm thinking through a concrete directory structure where stubs are organized by language and target file type — PHP stubs for backend artifacts like actions and models, React stubs for pages and components with their naming conventions, and native stubs for screens and navigators. Each stub maps to its output file extension through the generation logic. The rendering engine itself is language-agnostic and just handles token replacement and file writes, but the stubs embed language-specific knowledge. For React output, I need to consider Prettier formatting, the `@stackra/*` import paths, HeroUI Pro's compound component patterns, feature toggles from the manifest, and parallel i18n file generation. React Native adds Uniwind class names instead of Tailwind, the native component primitives, and Expo Router conventions. Before I finalize my response, I should verify what omniterm can actually do to give a solid recommendation on Prompts versus that approach, then walk through the concrete stub file structure and show example implementations for both React and Native to illustrate how the pattern works.
Let me look at omniterm before I give you a real comparison — I don't want to hand-wave.


3 tool calls
Fetch URL
https://github.com/pdphilip/omniterm
File Search
Check if omniterm is already vendored under .ref like the other references.
Fetch URL
https://github.com/pdphilip/omniterm

Thought complete
I'm realizing omniterm is actually a rendering engine rather than a prompt library—totally different purpose. Laravel Prompts handles the input side with interactive components like text fields, selections, confirmations, and progress indicators, and it's already integrated into Laravel core. Omniterm sits on the output side, providing rich terminal rendering like tables, badges, progress bars, and gradients through Tailwind-in-terminal conversion. They're complementary tools, not alternatives—I'd use Laravel Prompts for gathering user input and Omniterm for displaying polished output during long-running operations. The key distinction is that omniterm's `runTask()` and `liveTask()` methods offer more sophisticated async task runners with visual feedback compared to Laravel Prompts' simpler `spin()` function, so the mental model for our CLI is to ask questions with Laravel Prompts and show rich output with Omniterm. For the stub files question, the `.stub` rendering engine is language-agnostic—it's just token replacement and file writing—so I can organize stubs by language in separate directories (PHP, React, TypeScript, native, docs, config) and use the same uniform renderer across all of them. Each stub is tailored to its language, but the rendering pipeline stays consistent. I should also mention two critical post-generation steps: running formatters like Prettier or Pint on the emitted code, and then executing type checks or static analysis as a sanity gate before the generated files are used.
Both solve different problems — don't pick, use both.

Prompts vs Omniterm — different sides of the CLI
Concern	Laravel Prompts	Omniterm
Role	INPUT — interactive prompts	OUTPUT — terminal rendering
API surface	text(), password(), select(), multiselect(), confirm(), search(), suggest(), spin()	titleBar(), success()/error()/warn()/info() badges, tables, progress bars, liveTask(), gradients
Origin	Laravel core team; ships with laravel/framework	Built on the Termwind idea (Tailwind-in-terminal); adds truecolor + gradients + live tasks
Battle-tested by	laravel new, every php artisan make:* command	Newer, smaller install base
Non-TTY fallback	Automatic via Symfony's QuestionHelper	Prints raw text
Tailwind-y polish	No — plain colored output	Yes — HTML + Tailwind classes compiled to ANSI
Long-running task UX	Basic spin()	Rich liveTask() with live-updating rows
The Laravel Installer itself uses both patterns (Prompts for input + Termwind for output — omniterm is a modernized Termwind). Copy that split.

Concrete recommendation:

laravel/prompts for every user interaction — "What's the project name?", "Which capabilities?", "Confirm this package set?". Reliable, tested, graceful.
pdphilip/omniterm for the installer's output surface — the title bar, per-step status badges, progress bar as packages install, live task rows during module generation, tables that show which packages got picked.
Example flow inside a single command:

php

<?php

declare(strict_types=1);

namespace Stackra\Cli\Commands;

use Stackra\Cli\Catalog\CatalogQuery;
use OmniTerm\Async\Spinner;
use OmniTerm\HasOmniTerm;
use Symfony\Component\Console\Attribute\AsCommand;
use Symfony\Component\Console\Command\Command;
use Symfony\Component\Console\Input\InputInterface;
use Symfony\Component\Console\Output\OutputInterface;

use function Laravel\Prompts\{confirm, multiselect, select, text};

/**
 * `stackra new <name>` — scaffold a full Stackra project.
 *
 * Uses Laravel Prompts for the interview and OmniTerm for the
 * installer output. Both operate on the same Symfony Console
 * output stream — no coordination needed.
 *
 * @category Cli
 * @since    0.1.0
 */
#[AsCommand(name: 'new')]
final class NewProjectCommand extends Command
{
    use HasOmniTerm;

    public function __construct(
        private readonly CatalogQuery $catalog,
    ) {
        parent::__construct();
    }

    protected function execute(InputInterface $input, OutputInterface $output): int
    {
        // ── ASK (Laravel Prompts) ────────────────────────────────

        $name = text(
            label: 'Project name',
            placeholder: 'beauty-salon',
            required: true,
        );

        $businessType = select(
            label: 'Business shape',
            options: $this->catalog->businessTypes(),
        );

        $capabilities = multiselect(
            label: 'Capabilities',
            options: $this->catalog->capabilityGroups(),
            default: $this->catalog->defaultsFor($businessType),
        );

        $selection = $this->catalog->resolvePackages($capabilities);

        if (! confirm('Proceed with these packages?', default: true)) {
            $this->omni->warning('Aborted.');
            return Command::FAILURE;
        }

        // ── SHOW (OmniTerm) ──────────────────────────────────────

        $this->omni->titleBar("Bootstrapping {$name}", 'sky');

        $this->omni->tableHeader('Tier', 'Package', 'Reason');
        foreach ($selection->all() as $pkg) {
            $this->omni->tableRow($pkg->tier, $pkg->name, $pkg->reason);
        }

        $this->omni->hr();

        $this->omni->task(
            "Cloning app templates",
            fn () => ['state' => 'success', 'message' => "3 apps cloned"],
            Spinner::Dots3,
        );

        $bar = $this->omni->progressBar($selection->count())->framed()->steps();
        $bar->start();
        foreach ($selection->all() as $pkg) {
            $this->installPackage($pkg);
            $bar->advance();
        }
        $bar->finish();

        $this->omni->statusSuccess(
            'Project ready',
            "Run `cd {$name} && pnpm dev` to start.",
            ['Next: invoke `spec-intake-analyst` to draft the first feature.'],
        );

        return Command::SUCCESS;
    }
}
Add both to the CLI package's composer.json:

jsonc

{
  "require": {
    "php": "^8.3",
    "laravel/prompts": "^0.3",
    "pdphilip/omniterm": "^1.0",
    "symfony/console": "^7.0"
  }
}
Trade-off note: omniterm requires a modern terminal for truecolor. In CI (where terminals often report as dumb), it degrades to 256-color. Fine for our use case since the CLI is developer-facing, but if you ever want the same output rendered in a plain log file, use --no-ansi and both libraries fall back cleanly.

Stubs for frontend + native — yes, same mechanism
.stub files aren't PHP-specific. They're token-replacement templates over text files — the renderer doesn't care what language the output is. Same engine emits .php, .tsx, .ts, .json, .md, .yml, .mjs, whatever.

Concrete layout for our CLI:


backend-packages/foundation/cli/src/Stubs/
├── php/                     # backend Laravel emit
│   ├── action.stub
│   ├── model.stub
│   ├── interface.stub
│   ├── repository.stub
│   ├── data-input.stub      # Spatie Data with #[Required] etc.
│   ├── data-output.stub
│   ├── policy.stub
│   ├── migration.stub
│   ├── factory.stub
│   ├── seeder.stub
│   ├── event.stub
│   ├── listener.stub
│   ├── job.stub
│   ├── observer.stub
│   ├── enum-simple.stub
│   ├── enum-metadata.stub   # with #[Label] + #[Description]
│   ├── service-provider.stub
│   ├── command.stub
│   └── test-{feature,unit}.stub
│
├── react/                   # web frontend emit
│   ├── module-manifest.stub          → apps/*/src/modules/<domain>/<domain>.module.tsx
│   ├── page-list.stub                → pages/list.tsx
│   ├── page-create.stub              → pages/create.tsx
│   ├── page-edit.stub                → pages/edit.tsx
│   ├── page-show.stub                → pages/show.tsx
│   ├── component.stub                → *.component.tsx (HeroUI compound)
│   ├── hook.stub                     → use-*.hook.ts
│   ├── provider.stub                 → *.provider.tsx
│   ├── context.stub                  → *.context.ts
│   ├── interface.stub                → *.interface.ts (I-prefixed)
│   ├── constants.stub                → *.constants.ts
│   ├── form.stub                     → *-form.component.tsx
│   ├── data-grid-columns.stub        → *-columns.ts
│   ├── i18n-en.stub                  → src/core/i18n/en.json
│   ├── i18n-ar.stub                  → src/core/i18n/ar.json
│   └── barrel.stub                   → index.ts
│
├── native/                  # React Native emit
│   ├── screen.stub                   → *.screen.tsx (heroui-native-pro)
│   ├── component-native.stub         → *.component.tsx (Uniwind classes)
│   ├── hook-native.stub              → use-*.hook.ts
│   ├── navigator.stub                → *.navigator.tsx (Expo Router)
│   ├── form-native.stub              → *-form.component.tsx
│   └── list-native.stub              → *-list.screen.tsx (FlatList + heroui-native)
│
├── typescript/              # cross-cutting TS
│   ├── contracts-request.schema.stub   → docs/contracts/*-request.schema.json
│   ├── contracts-response.schema.stub  → docs/contracts/*-response.schema.json
│   ├── openapi-endpoint.stub           → openapi.yaml fragment
│   └── catalog-entry.stub              → catalog.json
│
├── python/                  # blueprint validation
│   └── blueprint-validator.stub        → modules/<name>/validate.py
│
└── docs/                    # markdown emit
    ├── adr.stub                      → docs/adr/NNNN-<slug>.md
    ├── package-readme.stub           → packages/*/README.md
    ├── runbook.stub                  → docs/runbooks/*.md
    ├── changelog.stub                → CHANGELOG.md
    └── release-notes.stub            → docs/release-notes/v*.md
The renderer is one class
Token replacement is dumb by design. One StubRenderer service reads a stub, swaps {{ tokens }}, writes to disk. Language-agnostic:

php

<?php

declare(strict_types=1);

namespace Stackra\Cli\Stubs;

use Illuminate\Container\Attributes\Scoped;
use Illuminate\Container\Attributes\Bind;
use Illuminate\Filesystem\Filesystem;

/**
 * Language-agnostic stub renderer.
 *
 * Reads a `.stub` file, substitutes `{{ token }}` markers with
 * values from a context map, writes the result to disk. The
 * emitted file's language (PHP / TSX / JSON / MD / YML) is
 * determined by the output path's extension — the renderer
 * itself doesn't care.
 *
 * Post-emit formatting is delegated to language-specific
 * formatters registered in `StubFormatterRegistry` (pint for
 * PHP, prettier for TSX / TS / JSON / MD, yamllint for YAML).
 *
 * @category Cli
 * @since    0.1.0
 */
#[Bind(StubRendererInterface::class)]
#[Scoped]
final class StubRenderer implements StubRendererInterface
{
    public function __construct(
        private readonly Filesystem $files,
        private readonly StubFormatterRegistry $formatters,
    ) {}

    /**
     * Render a stub to disk with the given token context.
     *
     * @param  string  $stub    Absolute path to the `.stub` template.
     * @param  string  $output  Absolute path the rendered file lands at.
     * @param  array<string, string|int|bool>  $context  Token map.
     *
     * @throws \RuntimeException  When the stub is missing or the output directory can't be created.
     */
    public function render(string $stub, string $output, array $context): void
    {
        $template = $this->files->get($stub);

        $rendered = preg_replace_callback(
            '/{{\s*([a-zA-Z0-9_]+)\s*}}/',
            static fn (array $m): string => (string) ($context[$m[1]] ?? "{{ MISSING:{$m[1]} }}"),
            $template,
        );

        $this->files->ensureDirectoryExists(\dirname($output));
        $this->files->put($output, $rendered);

        $this->formatters->for($output)?->format($output);
    }
}
Formatters register per extension:

.php → run pint --preset=laravel on the emitted file
.tsx / .ts / .mjs / .cjs / .js → run prettier --write
.json / .jsonc → prettier --parser=json
.md → prettier --parser=markdown
.yml / .yaml → prettier --parser=yaml
That means every generated file passes lint/format the moment it lands on disk. No two-step "generate then format" dance for the user.

Example react + native stubs
stubs/react/page-list.stub — matches the Refine + HeroUI Pro conventions in the frontend-module-architecture steering:

stub

/**
 * @file list.tsx
 * @module {{ appModule }}
 * @description List page for the {{ resource }} resource. Rendered
 *   by the {{ domain }} module manifest at `list` action.
 *   Generated by `stackra make:module {{ domain }}`.
 */

import { CanAccess } from "@refinedev/core";
import { ListView } from "@/components/refine";
import { ResourceDataGrid } from "@/components/refine";
import { {{ pascalResource }}Columns } from "@/modules/{{ domain }}/components/{{ kebabResource }}-columns";

/**
 * List view for {{ resource }} — page mounted at `{{ listRoute }}`.
 */
export default function {{ pascalResource }}List(): React.ReactElement {
  return (
    <CanAccess resource="{{ resource }}" action="list" fallback={<AccessDenied />}>
      <ListView resource="{{ resource }}">
        <ResourceDataGrid
          resource="{{ resource }}"
          columns={ {{ pascalResource }}Columns }
        />
      </ListView>
    </CanAccess>
  );
}
stubs/react/module-manifest.stub:

stub

/**
 * @file {{ domain }}.module.tsx
 * @module {{ appModule }}
 * @description AppModule manifest for the {{ domain }} feature.
 *   Auto-registered by the module registry glob at boot.
 *   Generated by `stackra make:module {{ domain }}`.
 */

import { {{ iconName }} } from "@stackra/ui/icons/outline";
import { createElement, lazy } from "react";
import type { AppModule } from "@/lib/module";

const {{ pascalResource }}ListPage = lazy(
  () => import("@/modules/{{ domain }}/pages/list"),
);
const {{ pascalResource }}ShowPage = lazy(
  () => import("@/modules/{{ domain }}/pages/show"),
);
const {{ pascalResource }}CreatePage = lazy(
  () => import("@/modules/{{ domain }}/pages/create"),
);
const {{ pascalResource }}EditPage = lazy(
  () => import("@/modules/{{ domain }}/pages/edit"),
);

const {{ camelDomain }}Module: AppModule = {
  name: "{{ domain }}",
  resources: [
    {
      name: "{{ resource }}",
      list: "{{ listRoute }}",
      show: "{{ showRoute }}",
      create: "{{ createRoute }}",
      edit: "{{ editRoute }}",
      meta: {
        label: "{{ label }}",
        icon: {{ iconName }},
        featureKey: "{{ featureKey }}",
        requiredPermission: "{{ resource }}.viewAny",
        scopedBy: [{{ scopedByList }}],
      },
    },
  ],
  routes: [
    { tier: "protected", path: "{{ listRoute }}",   element: createElement({{ pascalResource }}ListPage) },
    { tier: "protected", path: "{{ showRoute }}",   element: createElement({{ pascalResource }}ShowPage) },
    { tier: "protected", path: "{{ createRoute }}", element: createElement({{ pascalResource }}CreatePage) },
    { tier: "protected", path: "{{ editRoute }}",   element: createElement({{ pascalResource }}EditPage) },
  ],
};

export default {{ camelDomain }}Module;
stubs/native/screen.stub — HeroUI Native Pro + Uniwind + Expo Router:

stub

/**
 * @file {{ kebabResource }}.screen.tsx
 * @module {{ appModule }}
 * @description Native screen for the {{ resource }} resource.
 *   Generated by `stackra make:native-screen {{ resource }}`.
 */

import { Button, Card, Text, View } from "heroui-native-pro";
import { Stack, useRouter } from "expo-router";
import { useTranslation } from "@stackra/i18n/native";

/**
 * Native {{ label }} screen.
 */
export default function {{ pascalResource }}Screen(): React.ReactElement {
  const router = useRouter();
  const { t } = useTranslation("{{ domain }}");

  return (
    <View className="flex-1 bg-background p-4">
      <Stack.Screen options={ { title: t("{{ i18nKey }}.title") } } />

      <Card className="mb-4">
        <Card.Header>
          <Text className="text-lg font-semibold text-foreground">
            {t("{{ i18nKey }}.heading")}
          </Text>
        </Card.Header>
        <Card.Content>
          <Text className="text-sm text-foreground/70">
            {t("{{ i18nKey }}.body")}
          </Text>
        </Card.Content>
      </Card>

      <Button
        onPress={() => router.push("{{ nextRoute }}")}
        accessibilityLabel={t("{{ i18nKey }}.cta.aria")}
      >
        <Button.LabelContent>
          <Text>{t("{{ i18nKey }}.cta")}</Text>
        </Button.LabelContent>
      </Button>
    </View>
  );
}
stubs/react/i18n-en.stub and stubs/react/i18n-ar.stub are just plain JSON with {{ tokens }} — emitted in parallel to keep the two catalogs in sync.

The command that fans out to multi-language stubs
stackra module:new appointments doesn't just run one stub — it fans out to a set:


stackra module:new appointments
  ↓
Prompts (Laravel Prompts):
  ✔ Module name .......... appointments
  ✔ Tier ................. sports
  ✔ Business types ....... salon, gym, clinic
  ✔ Emit surfaces ........ php, react, native, i18n, docs
  ✔ Blueprint files ...... schemas/appointment.schema.json, ...

Emits (via StubRenderer, in parallel, then formatted):
  backend-packages/sports/appointments/
    ├── src/Contracts/Data/AppointmentInterface.php          (php/interface.stub)
    ├── src/Models/Appointment.php                           (php/model.stub)
    ├── src/Actions/Appointments/ListAppointments.php        (php/action.stub)
    ├── src/Actions/Appointments/CreateAppointment.php       (php/action.stub)
    ├── src/Actions/Appointments/UpdateAppointment.php       (php/action.stub)
    ├── src/Actions/Appointments/DeleteAppointment.php       (php/action.stub)
    ├── src/Data/CreateAppointmentData.php                   (php/data-input.stub)
    ├── src/Data/AppointmentData.php                         (php/data-output.stub)
    ├── src/Repositories/AppointmentRepository.php           (php/repository.stub)
    ├── src/Policies/AppointmentPolicy.php                   (php/policy.stub)
    ├── database/migrations/*_create_appointments_table.php  (php/migration.stub)
    ├── database/factories/AppointmentFactory.php            (php/factory.stub)
    ├── tests/Feature/ListAppointmentsTest.php               (php/test-feature.stub)
    ├── catalog.json                                         (typescript/catalog-entry.stub)
    └── README.md                                            (docs/package-readme.stub)

  apps/web/src/modules/appointments/
    ├── appointments.module.tsx                              (react/module-manifest.stub)
    ├── pages/list.tsx                                       (react/page-list.stub)
    ├── pages/create.tsx                                     (react/page-create.stub)
    ├── pages/edit.tsx                                       (react/page-edit.stub)
    ├── pages/show.tsx                                       (react/page-show.stub)
    ├── components/appointment-form.component.tsx            (react/form.stub)
    ├── components/appointments-columns.ts                   (react/data-grid-columns.stub)
    └── i18n/{en,ar}.json                                    (react/i18n-*.stub)

  apps/mobile/src/modules/appointments/
    ├── appointments.screen.tsx                              (native/screen.stub)
    ├── appointment-list.screen.tsx                          (native/list-native.stub)
    └── components/appointment-form.component.tsx            (native/form-native.stub)

  docs/contracts/
    ├── appointment-request.schema.json                      (typescript/contracts-request.schema.stub)
    └── appointment-response.schema.json                     (typescript/contracts-response.schema.stub)

  docs/adr/
    └── 00XX-appointments-scope.md                           (docs/adr.stub)

Post-emit:
  → pint on every .php
  → prettier on every .tsx / .ts / .json / .md
  → composer analyse on backend-packages/sports/appointments
  → pnpm typecheck on apps/web + apps/mobile
  → stackra module:validate appointments  (calls the Python validator)
stackra make:action <Name> is a subset of this — same renderer, single stub, no fan-out. Same for make:model, make:page, make:native-screen.

One thing to lock down
Prettier + Pint config live at the repo root and every generated file conforms — don't let the CLI ship its own formatting config. Otherwise you get drift between hand-authored and generated code. The generator is invisible when it emits code identical to what a human would write.

Recommendation
Use Laravel Prompts for input, Omniterm for output. Both in the same command.
.stub files handle every text-based output — PHP, TSX, TS, JSON, MD, YAML. One renderer, per-language stubs.
Post-emit formatting via the repo-root Pint + Prettier configs. Emitted code is indistinguishable from hand-authored code.
Fan-out from one CLI invocation to backend + web + native + contracts + docs in one shot. That's the "70% for free" moment the whole architecture is aimed at.
Want me to author the CLI package skeleton — composer.json + bin/stackra + one working command (stackra new) + three example stubs (php action, react page, native screen) — so you can see the shape land? That's a ~500-line concrete deliverable, and everything after is more of the same pattern.

}

Thought complete
The user wants a complete, production-ready CLI application with comprehensive documentation, organized using traits to separate concerns, and styled with ASCII art headers similar to Laravel's installer—matching that polished terminal aesthetic they've referenced. I'm planning the full package structure with Symfony Console as the foundation, pulling in Laravel Prompts for interactive input and OmniTerm for advanced terminal rendering. The package will live in `backend-packages/foundation/cli/` with a namespace of `Stackra\Cli`, and I'm laying out a substantial file structure with an abstract command base that composes all the necessary traits, individual command files, and supporting infrastructure for dependency injection and file operations. Continuing with the command classes and trait-based concerns... I'm finishing out the directory structure with the remaining support classes, exception hierarchy, and test files—roughly 40 files total. Now I need to prioritize the build order: start with the foundational pieces like composer.json, the bin entry point, and the Application container, then layer in the base Command class and shared traits before tackling the subsystems. For scope, I'm aiming for a genuinely usable CLI they can run today—all skeleton files with full docblocks, one complete end-to-end command (the `new` project command with ASCII art), working implementations of the stub renderer and catalog reader, a handful of concrete stubs to demonstrate the pattern, and the remaining commands as properly structured placeholders with clear extension points marked. The key constraints are comprehensive docblocks throughout, Symfony Console attributes for commands, horizontal trait organization, branded ASCII art for Stackra, and integration of both Laravel Prompts and OmniTerm as proposed. Now I'm thinking through what the Stackra logo should look like visually. Designing the ASCII banner... Defining gradient color palettes... Defining trait dependency resolution...

Task #1 is complete. Now I'm moving on to the core application files: Application.php, Container.php, and AbstractCommand.php. I need to think through these carefully since they form the foundation. Application.php will extend Symfony's Console Application class to handle versioning, container initialization, command registration, and display a branded banner before running commands. Container.php will set up an Illuminate Container instance with all the CLI services. The container won't be a full Laravel app—just a lightweight dependency injection container for the CLI's own needs, binding things like StubRenderer, CatalogReader, and TemplateManager. AbstractCommand.php will serve as the base class that composes all the traits, with concrete commands extending from it. Let me start writing these now.

Thought complete
Moving on to Task #7 now, which is building out the exception hierarchy. I'm creating a base CliException class with methods for headline, remediation, and exit codes, plus factory methods for each failure scenario I've encountered throughout the traits—things like missing files, invalid JSON, naming collisions, subprocess failures, and various validation errors for project and module names. I'm also defining four domain-specific subclasses: CatalogException for catalog-related failures, StubException for stub issues, TemplateException for template problems, and BlueprintException for blueprint and module validation errors. The key insight is that I can put the general factories on the base class while using the subclasses primarily for type-based exception catching, which keeps things organized without duplicating factory methods across every subclass. I'm now mapping out which factories belong where based on where they're actually called from in the traits.

Thought complete
Now I'm mapping out the catalog subsystem with four interconnected components: CatalogEntry as a read-only data transfer object for individual catalog.json files, CatalogReader that scans through the backend and packages directories to discover and aggregate these files into memory, and CatalogQuery which appears to be cut off but likely handles querying that aggregated catalog data. CatalogQuery provides higher-level lookups—searching by capability, resolving packages for multiple capabilities, applying business-type defaults, and organizing capabilities into groups. CatalogSelection then represents the deduplicated result set from a resolve operation, structured as a three-tier package collection. I'll implement these four files in parallel.

Thought complete
I need to implement ten command files for the application, with some already designed and others needing placeholders—starting with `NewProjectCommand` and `PackageAddCommand`, then moving through the module and make commands. I'm thinking about splitting this into two batches to keep the response manageable: first batch handles the four fully-implemented commands (`NewProjectCommand`, `MakeActionCommand`, `CatalogListCommand`, `CatalogSearchCommand`), then the second batch covers the six placeholder commands that'll display "coming soon" messages with clean documentation. For `NewProjectCommand`, I'm designing the flagship experience: it prompts for project name, business type, optional reference product URL, and capabilities (with smart defaults), then resolves packages, shows a confirmation table, clones three templates, installs dependencies, initializes git, and displays a success message. `MakeActionCommand` follows a simpler flow: verify we're in a Composer project, prompt for module and action details (name, HTTP verb, route), render the PHP action stub, and suggest next steps. `CatalogListCommand` reads the catalog, optionally filters by tier (frontend/backend/mobile), and renders an OmniTerm table with name, tier, kind, and purpose columns plus a summary count. `CatalogSearchCommand` takes a query string, searches by capability, displays matches in a table, and shows remediation guidance if nothing's found. For the remaining commands, I'm using a consistent placeholder pattern with an Attributes decorator, extending AbstractCommand, and returning a helpful message that describes what the command will do when it's fully implemented — that way users get guidance even from stub versions. I can fit all 10 commands across two responses: the first with the four full implementations (NewProject, MakeAction, CatalogList, CatalogSearch) at around 600 lines, then the six placeholders in a second response at around 300 lines total.

The full Day-0 → end-product path
Day -1 — stackra new my-salon → workspace on disk
Day 0 — Drop PDF → spec-intake-analyst → structured brief
Day 1-3 — product-lead → personas + PRD + INVEST stories
Day 4-7 — design-lead fans out 5 specialists → design package
Day 8-20 — delivery-lead fans out 11 builders → shipping code
Day 21-25 — quality-lead fans out 14 reviewers + 3 test/perf/a11y specialists → go/no-go
Day 26-30 — release-manager + deploy-engineer → tagged release + canary
Day 30+ — sre-lead + observability-engineer → SLIs + runbooks + dashboards
The three real gaps left to close before this pipeline runs a real feature end-to-end:

Author templates/{backend-app,web-app,mobile-app}/ at repo root so stackra new has something to clone.
Populate catalog.json across the ~68 packages under packages/** and backend-packages/** so the capability multiselect resolves real packages.

Thought complete
The user wants a status update on the CLI, so I'm reviewing what's been completed versus what remains. I've finished all 13 tasks from the todo list—the full package structure, core application logic, all the trait implementations, support classes, exceptions, and the four major subsystems plus ten commands. That's 57 files totaling nearly 7,700 lines of code. I've also wired this into the quickstart documentation and prepared the changeset. What's still pending breaks into three phases: first, the v0.1.0 substrate that the shipped code depends on—the three template directories that `stackra new` clones from, the catalog.json files across roughly 68 packages, and running composer install to pull runtime dependencies. Second, the immediate tooling to make the CLI usable—PATH wiring so the command is directly invocable and a smoke test to verify the banner and command list render properly. Third, the v0.2.0 work on the six placeholder commands: `package:add` for installing packages into existing apps, `module:new` for scaffolding blueprint modules with their JSON files and module-graph registration, `module:generate` for regenerating modules across all three platforms, and `make:model` for the four-file model quartet. Beyond that are the `make:page` command for full Refine page sets and `make:native-screen` for Expo Router screens, plus the test suite—currently empty despite phpunit.xml existing, though I'd want unit tests on the core subsystems and feature tests on the full commands. Further out are nice-to-haves like the JSON schema file that's referenced but doesn't exist yet, an `upgrade` command for self-updates, a `doctor` command for environment verification, and shell tab-completion support.

Command	What it will emit

make:page <resource>	Refine page set: list.tsx + create.tsx + edit.tsx + show.tsx + module manifest + column defs + form

module:generate <name>	Regenerate a module across backend + frontend + mobile from its blueprint
package:add <name>	Resolve against catalog + detect app tier + install via composer or pnpm + wire ServiceProvider or AppModule registration

php-attributes.md

Thought complete
I see that `OmniTerm\OmniTerm` is a trait, so I need to use composition rather than instantiation. The trait exposes a `$omni` property (an OmniHelpers instance) and an `initOmni()` method to set it up. I'm considering composing this trait directly into `AbstractCommand` so every command inherits the `$omni` helper and can call its methods like `$this->omni->success()`. But Option B is cleaner — I'll instantiate `OmniHelpers` directly in `UsesOmniTerm` and keep it private, so the wrapper stays self-contained without forcing the trait into `AbstractCommand`. Looking at the actual OmniHelpers API, I need to adjust my understanding. The constructor doesn't take any arguments, and the helper methods like `success()`, `error()`, `warning()`, and `info()` each accept just a message parameter with `success()` defaulting to 'ok'. For status methods, they take a title and details along with an optional help array for next steps. Table operations use `header()` and `row()` methods instead of the prefixed names I assumed, with variant methods like `rowSuccess()` and `rowError()` for styled rows. Progress tracking uses `createProgressBar()`, `showProgress()`, `progressAdvance()`, and `progressFinish()` rather than the simpler names I expected. I also don't have a `titleBar()` method—I'll need to use `box()` or `roundedBox()` instead. The `hr()` method takes a color parameter, and there's a `newLoader()` method for spinners that I hadn't accounted for.

access/* (5) — delegation, grants, invitations, rbac, requests
billing/* (2) — subscription, entitlements
finance/* (16) — invoice, refund, coupon, chargeback, transaction, tax, payment, membership, digital-passes, expenses

growth/* (5) — analytics, attribution, marketing, referrals

shared/* (10) — everything under shared/ except shared/foundation

platform/* (23) — tenancy, branding, storage, teams, staff, region, branch, facility, admin-console, ai, forms, integrations, ...
Tier 4 — DOMAIN / VERTICAL (24 pkgs) — SKIPPED for now
Sport-specific / product-specific — not portable across business types.

sports/* (21), products/* (1), workflow/* (2)

How to physically organize — Option A wins
Two ways to reflect the tiers in the filesystem:

Option A (recommended)	Option B (rejected)
Approach	Keep current top-level layout, add tier field to catalog.json	Move everything under foundation-tier/, framework-tier/, saas-tier/, domain-tier/
Cost	Zero file moves. Zero composer path breakage. Zero autoload changes.	~120 renames. Every app's composer.json path repository breaks. Every existing PHP use statement's PSR-4 path breaks.
Benefit	CLI filters at query time (stackra catalog:list --tier framework)	Visual layout matches semantic layout
Option A wins. The current top-level names (foundation/, framework/, identity/, sports/) already imply the tier. The catalog metadata + CLI filter is the missing piece.

Package	My call	Alternative	Reasoning
authorization/authorization	Framework	SaaS	Permission substrate the SaaS access/* sits on. Framework because it's a primitive; SaaS because it's a product feature.
compliance/architecture	Framework	SaaS	ADR-rule linter (tooling), not a customer-facing compliance product.
compliance/compliance	SaaS	Framework	Customer-facing GDPR/PDPL flows.
platform/* (23 pkgs)	SaaS	Framework	Tenancy + region + branch + facility are SaaS-shape opinions (multi-tenant, hierarchical).
growth/*	SaaS	Domain	Analytics + attribution are cross-vertical SaaS mechanics.
sdk/*	SaaS	Framework	Client libraries wrapping cross-cutting SaaS APIs.

Est. Credits Used: 6.67 (?)

Kiro

Thought complete
Considering whether to publish the JSON schemas as GitHub gists instead of keeping them in the repo — the main appeal is that the `$schema` field could point directly to a gist URL, which tools and IDEs would resolve without needing a local clone. But there are real downsides: gists version independently from the code, so schema and implementation can drift silently; every developer and CI pipeline needs network access to validate, which breaks airgapped builds; gists are tied to individual GitHub accounts and can disappear during team transitions; and schemas hidden on gist.github.com aren't discoverable by someone reading the repo. Plus, schema changes can't be reviewed in PRs if they live on a gist. The actual blocker was just the `fs_write` tool's policy against remote JSON schemas in supervised mode — dropping the `$schema` field is a perfectly valid workaround since JSON Schema Draft 2020-12 is the implicit default. The CLI doesn't even use `$schema` for validation; it reads `catalog.json` files directly from disk. The schema's real purpose is documentation and IDE autocomplete, which works fine without the field. I should recommend keeping schemas as physical files in the repo instead: they get reviewed alongside code changes, work offline, stay versioned together with the code that reads them, and are discoverable by anyone browsing the repo. If we need IDE autocomplete from a URL later, we can configure VS Code's `settings.json` to map catalog files to local schemas, or publish to a GitHub release asset URL — both options keep everything repo-tracked without the gist coupling. Let me give them a direct recommendation and then ask if they want me to proceed with the agent work that got interrupted.
Short answer: keep them as physical files in the repo. Gists are the wrong tool here.

Why physical files win:

PR-reviewable — schema changes go through the same review as the code that reads them. On a gist, a breaking schema edit lands with no diff visible in the PR.
Versioned together — the schema and CatalogEntry.php (which validates against it) evolve as one commit. On a gist they can drift silently.
Offline / airgapped works — no raw.githubusercontent.com roundtrip during dev + CI.
Ownership stable — a gist is tied to whoever ran gh gist create; rotating that person is a supply-chain break. A repo file is owned by the org.
Discoverable — .ref/schemas/ is greppable in the codebase. A gist URL is a mystery box unless someone remembers it.
The CLI doesn't use $schema at runtime anyway — CatalogReader reads catalog.json files by shape, not by validating against a URL.
The $schema field I dropped from catalog.v1.json earlier was the trigger for the "Remote JSON Schema" write-guard. The file still functions as a JSON Schema — Draft 2020-12 is the implicit default when the $schema key is omitted. You lose zero validation power.

If you want IDE autocomplete on catalog.json files (the one thing a URL-published schema gives you), do it with a workspace hint instead:

jsonc

// .vscode/settings.json
{
  "json.schemas": [
    {
      "fileMatch": ["**/catalog.json"],
      "url": "./.ref/schemas/catalog.v1.json"
    }
  ]
}
Same autocomplete + validation in VS Code, still 100% offline, still repo-owned.

If we DO want a URL later (e.g. for a schema registry, openapi.yaml $ref from an external doc), the right move is a GitHub release asset — pinned to a tag, still repo-tracked, immutable per release. Not a mutable gist.

Sticking with .ref/schemas/catalog.v1.json as the source of truth. Ready to fire the 5 sub-agents against it — want me to proceed?

I'm thinking through the tier structure: foundation packages as Tier 1, framework and telemetry as Tier 2, and everything else as Tier 3. Given the volume of catalog files needed, I'm considering how to generate them efficiently—likely using a programmatic approach rather than writing each one individually. I'll create a single script file that generates all 108 catalog.json files plus the 5 skip-bucket READMEs in one go, execute it, then clean it up—that's the most efficient way to handle this.
Now for Deliverable 2. Given 108 catalog.json files, I'll write a Python script to generate them from a structured spec, run it, then remove it.

Thought complete
I'm ready to generate the stub files and catalog entries, starting with the token naming conventions that map PHP action patterns to TypeScript equivalents like namespace, class name, and description fields. For the catalog.json stub specifically, I need to handle the JSON schema constraint that forbids additional properties — so I can't add `_comment` fields to the final output. The approach is to include explanatory comments in the stub template itself, which the renderer will strip out when producing the actual catalog.json files that consumers will use.

1 tool call
Read Files

StubRenderer.php

StubFormatter.php

StubRegistry.php

Thought complete
The CLI renders stubs through token substitution, so comments embedded in the stubs pass through unchanged to the output. For JSON files like catalog.json, I'm using `_comment` fields as a workaround—they're valid JSON even though they technically violate the schema's `additionalProperties: false` constraint, but since this stub is meant for human consumption anyway, it's acceptable. Reading the task more carefully though, it specifies that JSON stubs should use `//` line comments for JSONC-tolerant files or `_comment` fields otherwise. Since catalog.json isn't JSONC-tolerant, `_comment` fields are the right choice. The tension is that when the stub gets rendered into an actual catalog.json, those `_comment` fields would violate the schema—so I need to decide whether to include explanatory `_comment` fields that consumers manually strip out, or keep comments minimal and rely on a docblock header in the stub itself. I'm going to go with `//` line comments in the stub file (treating it as JSON5 for authorship convenience) and add a top-of-file header explaining that consumers need to strip the comments before saving as valid JSON. Since the `.stub` extension isn't matched by prettier's JSON parser, the comments will stay intact in the template. Now I need to write out the ten stubs, and I should check what existing config patterns look like in the packages to make sure I'm modeling them correctly.

3 tool calls
Command
/Users/akouta/Projects/stackra-frontend
node -e "
const schema = require('./.ref/schemas/catalog.v1.json');
const fs = require('node:fs');
const path = require('node:path');

const roots = fs.readdirSync('packages', { withFileTypes: true })
  .filter(d => d.isDirectory())
  .map(d => path.join('packages', d.name, 'catalog.json'))
  .filter(p => fs.existsSync(p));

let bad = 0;
for (const p of roots) {
  const raw = JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync(p, 'utf8'));
  const missing = schema.required.filter(k => !(k in raw));
  const extra = Object.keys(raw).filter(k => !(k in schema.properties));
  const badTier = !schema.properties.tier.enum.includes(raw.tier);
  const badKind = raw.kind && !schema.properties.kind.enum.includes(raw.kind);
  const badMat = raw.maturity && !schema.properties.maturity.enum.includes(raw.maturity);
  const badNamePat = !/^([a-z0-9-]+\/[a-z0-9-]+|@[a-z0-9-]+\/[a-z0-9-]+)\$/.test(raw.name);
  const badSurfaces = (raw.surfaces || []).filter(s => !schema.properties.surfaces.items.enum.includes(s));
  const problems = [];
  if (missing.length) problems.push('missing=' + missing.join(','));
  if (extra.length) problems.push('extra=' + extra.join(','));
  if (badTier) problems.push('bad-tier=' + raw.tier);
  if (badKind) problems.push('bad-kind=' + raw.kind);
  if (badMat) problems.push('bad-maturity=' + raw.maturity);
  if (badNamePat) problems.push('bad-name-pattern=' + raw.name);
  if (badSurfaces.length) problems.push('bad-surfaces=' + badSurfaces.join(','));
  if (raw.purpose && raw.purpose.length > 200) problems.push('purpose-too-long=' + raw.purpose.length);
  if (problems.length) { console.log('FAIL', p, problems.join(' ')); bad++; }
  else console.log('OK  ', p);
}
process.exit(bad ? 1 : 0);
"

I'm settling on the strict interpretation: always include core and react, add native only if that directory exists, and testing gets included if present since it's part of the probe list. Now I need to verify the src structure for kbd and check the workspace configuration before diving into the stub generation, then I'll start writing out the 17 React stubs following the existing template pattern with JSDoc headers, minimal imports, and token markers. For the module manifest stub specifically, it should use a default export like the page stubs do, since that's how the registry glob-loads them — same exception pattern as the Refine pages. I'm mapping out the token set I'll need across all stubs: domain, pascalDomain, resource, pascalResource, kebabResource, listRoute, i18nKey, and others depending on the stub type. Now walking through each of the 17 stubs: page-create, page-edit, and page-show are all Refine pages that compose their respective view components with form or field list components. The form stub is a controlled component using Refine's useForm hook with HeroUI fields. Data grid columns is just an array of column definitions. The hook stub is a standard React hook with typed inputs and outputs. For the provider stub, I'm thinking about the three-file pattern from the architecture docs — the stub itself is just the provider component, which would import from sibling context and hook files that the CLI scaffolds separately. Then there's the i18n stubs for English and Arabic catalogs, followed by the backend stubs: a DI module, an injectable service, a registry extending BaseRegistry, event mapping and payload interfaces, an error boundary fallback component, and finally a HeroUI compound component. Before I finalize these, I need to verify the HeroUI compound APIs I'm referencing — specifically checking that Card, ComboBox, and other components have the subcomponent structure I'm planning to use. {"response":"# card Documentation (@heroui/react)\n\n**URL:** https://heroui.com/docs/react/components/card.mdx\n\n---\n\n<page url=\"/en/docs/react/components/card\">\n# Card\n\n**Category**: react\n**URL**: https://www.heroui.com/en/docs/react/components/card\n**Source**: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/heroui-inc/heroui/refs/heads/v3/apps/docs/content/docs/en/react/components/(layout)/card.mdx\n> Flexible container component for grouping related content and actions\n\n\n## Import\n\n```tsx\nimport { Card } from \"@heroui/react\";\n\n```\n\n### Usage\n\n```tsx\nimport {CircleDollar} from \"@gravity-ui/icons\";\nimport {Card, Link} from \"@heroui/react\";\n\nexport function Default() {\n  return (\n    <Card className=\"w-[400px]\">\n      <CircleDollar aria-label=\"Dollar sign icon\" className=\"text-primary size-6\" role=\"img\" />\n      <Card.Header>\n        <Card.Title>Become an Acme Creator!</Card.Title>\n        <Card.Description>\n          Visit the Acme Creator Hub to sign up today and start earning credits from your fans and\n          followers.\n        </Card.Description>\n      </Card.Header>\n      <Card.Footer>\n        <Link\n          aria-label=\"Go to Acme Creator Hub (opens in new tab)\"\n          href=\"https://heroui.com\"\n          rel=\"noopener noreferrer\"\n          target=\"_blank\"\n        >\n          Creator Hub\n          <Link.Icon aria-hidden=\"true\" />\n        </Link>\n      </Card.Footer>\n    </Card>\n  );\n}\n\n```\n\n### Anatomy\n\nImport the Card component and access all parts using dot notation.\n\n```tsx\nimport { Card } from \"@heroui/react\";\n\nexport default () => (\n  <Card>\n    <Card.Header>\n      <Card.Title />\n      <Card.Description />\n    </Card.Header>\n    <Card.Content />\n    <Card.Footer />\n  </Card>\n);\n\n```\n\n### Variants\n\nCards come in semantic variants that describe their prominence level rather than specific visual styles. This allows themes to interpret them differently:\n\n```tsx\nimport {Card} from \"@heroui/react\";\n\nexport function Variants() {\n  return (\n    <div className=\"flex flex-col gap-4\">\n      <Card className=\"w-[320px]\" variant=\"transparent\">\n        <Card.Header>\n          <Card.Title>Transparent</Card.Title>\n          <Card.Description>Minimal prominence with transparent background</Card.Description>\n        </Card.Header>\n        <Card.Content>\n          <p>Use for less important content or nested cards</p>\n        </Card.Content>\n      </Card>\n\n      <Card className=\"w-[320px]\" variant=\"default\">\n        <Card.Header>\n          <Card.Title>Default</Card.Title>\n          <Card.Description>Standard card appearance (bg-surface)</Card.Description>\n        </Card.Header>\n        <Card.Content>\n          <p>The default card variant for most use cases</p>\n        </Card.Content>\n      </Card>\n\n      <Card className=\"w-[320px]\" variant=\"secondary\">\n        <Card.Header>\n          <Card.Title>Secondary</Card.Title>\n          <Card.Description>Medium prominence (bg-surface-secondary)</Card.Description>\n        </Card.Header>\n        <Card.Content>\n          <p>Use to draw moderate attention</p>\n        </Card.Content>\n      </Card>\n\n      <Card className=\"w-[320px]\" variant=\"tertiary\">\n        <Card.Header>\n          <Card.Title>Tertiary</Card.Title>\n          <Card.Description>Higher prominence (bg-surface-tertiary)</Card.Description>\n        </Card.Header>\n        <Card.Content>\n          <p>Use for primary or featured content</p>\n        </Card.Content>\n      </Card>\n    </div>\n  );\n}\n\n```\n\n* **`transparent`** - Minimal prominence, transparent background (great for nested cards)\n* **`default`** - Standard card for most use cases (surface-secondary)\n* **`secondary`** - Medium prominence to draw moderate attention (surface-tertiary)\n* **`tertiary`** - Higher prominence for important content (surface-tertiary)\n\n### Horizontal Layout\n\n```tsx\nimport {Button, Card, CloseButton} from \"@heroui/react\";\n\nexport function Horizontal() {\n  return (\n    <Card className=\"w-full items-stretch md:flex-row\">\n      <div className=\"relative h-[140px] w-full shrink-0 overflow-hidden rounded-2xl sm:h-[120px] sm:w-[120px]\">\n        <img\n          alt=\"Cherries\"\n          className=\"pointer-events-none absolute inset-0 h-full w-full scale-125 object-cover select-none\"\n          loading=\"lazy\"\n          src=\"https://heroui-assets.nyc3.cdn.digitaloceanspaces.com/docs/cherries.jpeg\"\n        />\n      </div>\n      <div className=\"flex flex-1 flex-col gap-3\">\n        <Card.Header className=\"gap-1\">\n          <Card.Title className=\"pr-8\">Become an ACME Creator!</Card.Title>\n          <Card.Description>\n            Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet consectetur. Sed arcu donec id aliquam dolor sed amet\n            faucibus etiam.\n          </Card.Description>\n          <CloseButton aria-label=\"Close banner\" className=\"absolute top-3 right-3\" />\n        </Card.Header>\n        <Card.Footer className=\"mt-auto flex w-full flex-col items-start gap-3 sm:flex-row sm:items-center sm:justify-between\">\n          <div className=\"flex flex-col\">\n            <span className=\"text-sm font-medium text-foreground\">Only 10 spots</span>\n            <span className=\"text-xs text-muted\">Submission ends Oct 10.</span>\n          </div>\n          <Button className=\"w-full sm:w-auto\">Apply Now</Button>\n        </Card.Footer>\n      </div>\n    </Card>\n  );\n}\n\n```\n\n### With Avatar\n\n```tsx\nimport {Avatar, Card} from \"@heroui/react\";\n\nexport function WithAvatar() {\n  return (\n    <div className=\"flex flex-wrap gap-4\">\n      <Card className=\"w-[200px] gap-2\">\n        <img\n          alt=\"Indie Hackers community\"\n          className=\"pointer-events-none aspect-square w-14 rounded-2xl object-cover select-none\"\n          loading=\"lazy\"\n          src=\"https://heroui-assets.nyc3.cdn.digitaloceanspaces.com/docs/demo1.jpg\"\n        />\n        <Card.Header>\n          <Card.Title>Indie Hackers</Card.Title>\n          <Card.Description>148 members</Card.Description>\n        </Card.Header>\n        <Card.Footer className=\"flex gap-2\">\n          <Avatar aria-label=\"Martha's profile picture\" className=\"size-5\">\n            <Avatar.Image\n              alt=\"Martha's avatar\"\n              src=\"https://heroui-assets.nyc3.cdn.digitaloceanspaces.com/avatars/red.jpg\"\n            />\n            <Avatar.Fallback className=\"text-xs\">IH</Avatar.Fallback>\n          </Avatar>\n          <span className=\"text-xs\">By Martha</span>\n        </Card.Footer>\n      </Card>\n\n      <Card className=\"w-[200px] gap-2\">\n        <img\n          alt=\"AI Builders community\"\n          className=\"pointer-events-none aspect-square w-14 rounded-2xl object-cover select-none\"\n          loading=\"lazy\"\n          src=\"https://heroui-assets.nyc3.cdn.digitaloceanspaces.com/docs/demo2.jpg\"\n        />\n        <Card.Header>\n          <Card.Title>AI Builders</Card.Title>\n          <Card.Description>362 members</Card.Description>\n        </Card.Header>\n        <Card.Footer className=\"flex gap-2\">\n          <Avatar aria-label=\"John's profile picture\" className=\"size-5\">\n            <Avatar.Image\n              alt=\"John's avatar - blue themed\"\n              src=\"https://heroui-assets.nyc3.cdn.digitaloceanspaces.com/avatars/blue.jpg\"\n            />\n            <Avatar.Fallback className=\"text-xs\">B</Avatar.Fallback>\n          </Avatar>\n          <span className=\"text-xs\">By John</span>\n        </Card.Footer>\n      </Card>\n    </div>\n  );\n}\n\n```\n\n### With Images\n\n```tsx\nimport {CircleDollar} from \"@gravity-ui/icons\";\nimport {Avatar, Button, Card, CloseButton, Link} from \"@heroui/react\";\n\nexport function WithImages() {\n  return (\n    <div className=\"flex w-full items-center justify-center\">\n      <div className=\"grid w-full max-w-2xl grid-cols-12 gap-4 p-4\">\n        {/* Row 1: Large Product Card - Available Soon */}\n        <Card className=\"col-span-12 flex h-auto min-h-[152px] flex-col sm:flex-row\">\n          <div className=\"relative h-[140px] w-full shrink-0 overflow-hidden rounded-2xl sm:h-[120px] sm:w-[120px]\">\n            <img\n              alt=\"Cherries\"\n              className=\"pointer-events-none absolute inset-0 h-full w-full scale-125 object-cover select-none\"\n {"response":"# HeroUI Native Components (Unified)\n\n> This MCP covers both `heroui-native-pro` (Pro) and `heroui-native` (OSS). Import from the correct package.\n\n## `heroui-native-pro` (Pro)\n\n### Buttons\n- fab\n- progress-button\n- slide-button\n- social-auth-button\n- toggle-button\n- toggle-button-group\n\n### Charts\n- area-chart\n- bar-chart\n- chart-crosshair\n- chart-indicator\n- chart-tooltip\n- composed-chart\n- line-chart\n- pie-chart\n- radar-chart\n- radial-chart\n\n### Data Display\n- badge\n- empty-state\n- flip-card\n- timeline\n- widget\n\n### Date and Time\n- calendar\n- date-field\n- date-picker\n- date-range-picker\n- date-time-picker\n- range-calendar\n- time-picker\n- wheel-date-time-picker\n- wheel-time-picker\n\n### Feedback\n- number-value\n- progress-bar\n- progress-circle\n- rating\n- trend-chip\n\n### Forms\n- number-stepper\n- number-field\n- number-pad\n- radio-button-group\n- wheel-picker\n- wheel-picker-group\n\n### Navigation\n- segment\n- stepper\n- split-view\n\n**Pro total:** 44 components\n\n---\n\n## `heroui-native` (OSS)\n\n- accordion\n- alert\n- avatar\n- bottom-sheet\n- button\n- card\n- checkbox\n- chip\n- close-button\n- control-field\n- description\n- dialog\n- field-error\n- input\n- input-group\n- input-otp\n- label\n- link-button\n- list-group\n- menu\n- popover\n- pressable-feedback\n- radio-group\n- scroll-shadow\n- search-field\n- select\n- separator\n- skeleton\n- skeleton-group\n- slider\n- spinner\n- surface\n- switch\n- tabs\n- tag-group\n- text\n- text-area\n- text-field\n- toast\n\n**OSS total:** 39 components\n\n---\n\n**Combined total:** 83 components\n\nUse `get_component_docs` with any name from either list to get full documentation.","imageBase64Urls":[]}
