{"response":"# card Documentation (heroui-native)\n\n**URL:** https://heroui.com/docs/native/components/card.mdx\n\n---\n\n<page url=\"/en/docs/native/components/card\">\n# Card\n\n**Category**: native\n**URL**: https://www.heroui.com/en/docs/native/components/card\n**Source**: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/heroui-inc/heroui/refs/heads/v3/apps/docs/content/docs/en/native/components/(layout)/card.mdx\n> Displays a card container with flexible layout sections for structured content.\n\n\n\n\n## Import\n\n```tsx\nimport { Card } from 'heroui-native';\n\n```\n\n## Anatomy\n\n```tsx\n<Card>\n  <Card.Header>...</Card.Header>\n  <Card.Body>\n    <Card.Title>...</Card.Title>\n    <Card.Description>...</Card.Description>\n  </Card.Body>\n  <Card.Footer>...</Card.Footer>\n</Card>\n\n```\n\n* **Card**: Main container that extends Surface component. Provides base card structure with configurable surface variants and handles overall layout.\n* **Card.Header**: Header section for top-aligned content like icons or badges.\n* **Card.Body**: Main content area with flex-1 that expands to fill all available space between Card.Header and Card.Footer.\n* **Card.Title**: Title text with foreground color and medium font weight.\n* **Card.Description**: Description text with muted color and smaller font size.\n* **Card.Footer**: Footer section for bottom-aligned actions like buttons.\n\n## Usage\n\n### Basic Usage\n\nThe Card component creates a container with built-in sections for organized content.\n\n```tsx\n<Card>\n  <Card.Body>...</Card.Body>\n</Card>\n\n```\n\n### With Title and Description\n\nCombine title and description components for structured text content.\n\n```tsx\n<Card>\n  <Card.Body>\n    <Card.Title>...</Card.Title>\n    <Card.Description>...</Card.Description>\n  </Card.Body>\n</Card>\n\n```\n\n### With Header and Footer\n\nAdd header and footer sections for icons, badges, or actions.\n\n```tsx\n<Card>\n  <Card.Header>...</Card.Header>\n  <Card.Body>...</Card.Body>\n  <Card.Footer>...</Card.Footer>\n</Card>\n\n```\n\n### Variants\n\nControl the card's background appearance using different variants.\n\n```tsx\n<Card variant=\"default\">...</Card>\n<Card variant=\"secondary\">...</Card>\n<Card variant=\"tertiary\">...</Card>\n<Card variant=\"transparent\">...</Card>\n\n```\n\n### Horizontal Layout\n\nCreate horizontal cards by using flex-row styling.\n\n```tsx\n<Card className=\"flex-row gap-4\">\n  <Image source={...} className=\"size-24 rounded-lg\" />\n</Card>\n\n```\n\n### Background Image\n\nUse an image as an absolute positioned background.\n\n```tsx\n<Card>\n  <Image source={...} className=\"absolute inset-0\" />\n  <View className=\"gap-4\">...</View>\n</Card>\n\n```\n\n## Example\n\n```tsx\nimport { Button, Card } from 'heroui-native';\nimport { Ionicons } from '@expo/vector-icons';\nimport { View } from 'react-native';\n\nexport default function CardExample() {\n  return (\n    <Card>\n      <View className=\"gap-4\">\n        <Card.Body className=\"mb-4\">\n          <View className=\"gap-1 mb-2\">\n            <Card.Title className=\"text-pink-500\">$450</Card.Title>\n            <Card.Title>Living room Sofa • Collection 2025</Card.Title>\n          </View>\n          <Card.Description>\n            This sofa is perfect for modern tropical spaces, baroque inspired\n            spaces.\n          </Card.Description>\n        </Card.Body>\n        <Card.Footer className=\"gap-3\">\n          <Button variant=\"primary\">Buy now</Button>\n          <Button variant=\"ghost\">\n            <Button.Label>Add to cart</Button.Label>\n            <Ionicons name=\"bag-outline\" size={16} />\n          </Button>\n        </Card.Footer>\n      </View>\n    </Card>\n  );\n}\n\n```\n\nYou can find more examples in the [GitHub repository](https://github.com/heroui-inc/heroui-native/blob/main/example/src/app/\\(home\\)/components/card.tsx).\n\n## API Reference\n\n### Card\n\n| prop           | type                                                      | default     | description                                                                               |\n| -------------- | --------------------------------------------------------- | ----------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |\n| `children`     | `React.ReactNode`                                         | -           | Content to be rendered inside the card                                                    |\n| `variant`      | `'default' \\| 'secondary' \\| 'tertiary' \\| 'transparent'` | `'default'` | Visual variant of the card surface                                                        |\n| `className`    | `string`                                                  | -           | Additional CSS classes to apply                                                           |\n| `animation`    | `\"disable-all\" \\| undefined`                              | `undefined` | Animation configuration. Use `\"disable-all\"` to disable all animations including children |\n| `...ViewProps` | `ViewProps`                                               | -           | All standard React Native View props are supported                                        |\n\n### Card.Header\n\n| prop           | type              | default | description                                        |\n| -------------- | ----------------- | ------- | -------------------------------------------------- |\n| `children`     | `React.ReactNode` | -       | Children elements to be rendered inside the header |\n| `className`    | `string`          | -       | Additional CSS classes                             |\n| `...ViewProps` | `ViewProps`       | -       | All standard React Native View props are supported |\n\n### Card.Body\n\n| prop           | type              | default | description                                        |\n| -------------- | ----------------- | ------- | -------------------------------------------------- |\n| `children`     | `React.ReactNode` | -       | Children elements to be rendered inside the body   |\n| `className`    | `string`          | -       | Additional CSS classes                             |\n| `...ViewProps` | `ViewProps`       | -       | All standard React Native View props are supported |\n\n### Card.Footer\n\n| prop           | type              | default | description                                        |\n| -------------- | ----------------- | ------- | -------------------------------------------------- |\n| `children`     | `React.ReactNode` | -       | Children elements to be rendered inside the footer |\n| `className`    | `string`          | -       | Additional CSS classes                             |\n| `...ViewProps` | `ViewProps`       | -       | All standard React Native View props are supported |\n\n### Card.Title\n\n| prop           | type              | default | description                                        |\n| -------------- | ----------------- | ------- | -------------------------------------------------- |\n| `children`     | `React.ReactNode` | -       | Children elements to be rendered as the title text |\n| `className`    | `string`          | -       | Additional CSS classes                             |\n| `...TextProps` | `TextProps`       | -       | All standard React Native Text props are supported |\n\n### Card.Description\n\n| prop           | type              | default | description                                              |\n| -------------- | ----------------- | ------- | -------------------------------------------------------- |\n| `children`     | `React.ReactNode` | -       | Children elements to be rendered as the description text |\n| `className`    | `string`          | -       | Additional CSS classes                                   |\n| `...TextProps` | `TextProps`       | -       | All standard React Native Text props are supported       |\n\n</page>\n\n---\n\n# text-field Documentation (heroui-native)\n\n**URL:** https://heroui.com/docs/native/components/text-field.mdx\n\n---\n\n<page url=\"/en/docs/native/components/text-field\">\n# TextField\n\n**Category**: native\n**URL**: https://www.heroui.com/en/docs/native/components/text-field\n**Source**: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/heroui-inc/heroui/refs/heads/v3/apps/docs/content/docs/en/native/components/(forms)/text-field.mdx\n> A text input component with label, description, and error handling for collecting user input.\n\n\n\n\n## Import\n\n```tsx\nimport { TextField } from 'heroui-native';\n\n```\n\n## Anatomy\n\n```tsx\n<TextField>\n  <Label>...</Label>\n  <Input />\n  <Description>...</Description>\n  <FieldError>...</FieldError>\n</TextField>\n\n```\n\n* **TextField**: Root container that provides spacing and state management\n* **Label**: Label with optional asterisk for required fields (from [Label](./label) component)\n* **Input**: Input container with animated border and background (from [Input](./input) component)\n* **Description**: Secondary descriptive helper text (from [Description](./description) component)\n* **FieldError**: Validation error message display (from [FieldError](./field-error) component)\n\n## Usage\n\n### Basic Usage\n\nTextField provides a complete form input structure with label and description.\n\n```tsx\n<TextField>\n  <Label>Email</Label>\n  <Input placeholder=\"Enter your email\" />\n  <Description>We'll never share your email</Description>\n</TextField>\n\n```\n\n### With Required Field\n\nMark fields as required to show an asterisk in the label.\n\n```tsx\n<TextField isRequired>\n  <Label>Username</Label>\n  <Input placeholder=\"Choose a username\" />\n</TextField>\n\n```\n\n### With Validation\n\nDisplay error messages when the field is invalid.\n\n```tsx\nimport { FieldError, Input, Label, TextField } from 'heroui-native';\n\n<TextField isRequired isInvalid={true}>\n  <Label>Email</Label>\n  <Input placeholder=\"Enter your email\" />\n  <FieldE

{"response":"# date-picker Documentation (heroui-native-pro)\n\n---\n\n<page url=\"/native/components/date-picker\">\n# DatePicker\n\n**Category**: native\n**URL**: https://docs.heroui.pro/native/components/date-picker\n**Source**: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/heroui-inc/heroui/refs/heads/main/apps/docs/content/docs/native/components/(date-and-time)/date-picker.mdx\n> A single-date picker that combines a trigger field with a calendar popup for selecting dates.\n\n\n> `DatePicker` uses [`@internationalized/date`](https://react-aria.adobe.com/internationalized/date/) for all date manipulations (`CalendarDate`, calendar systems, time zones, locale-aware formatting). For full context on the date types and helpers exposed through this component's props and callbacks, read the [`@internationalized/date` docs](https://react-aria.adobe.com/internationalized/date/) alongside this page.\n\n## Import\n\n```tsx\nimport { DatePicker } from 'heroui-native-pro';\n\n```\n\n## Anatomy\n\n```tsx\n<DatePicker>\n  <Label>...</Label>\n  <DatePicker.Select>\n    <DatePicker.Trigger>\n      <DatePicker.Value />\n      <DatePicker.TriggerIndicator />\n    </DatePicker.Trigger>\n    <DatePicker.Portal>\n      <DatePicker.Overlay />\n      <DatePicker.Content>\n        <DatePicker.Calendar>\n          <Calendar.Header>...</Calendar.Header>\n          <Calendar.Grid>...</Calendar.Grid>\n        </DatePicker.Calendar>\n      </DatePicker.Content>\n    </DatePicker.Portal>\n  </DatePicker.Select>\n  <Description>...</Description>\n</DatePicker>\n\n```\n\n* **DatePicker**: Root container that manages date selection state, open state, display formatting, and form field context (for Label, Description, FieldError). Supports controlled and uncontrolled modes.\n* **DatePicker.Select**: Pre-wired Select root connected to the DatePicker context. State props (`value`, `isOpen`, `onValueChange`, `onOpenChange`) are managed by the root.\n* **DatePicker.Trigger**: Pressable trigger button that opens the calendar overlay. Supports `variant` and inherits invalid border styling from the root.\n* **DatePicker.Value**: Text display for the selected date label. Shows a placeholder when no date is selected.\n* **DatePicker.TriggerIndicator**: Indicator icon inside the trigger. Defaults to a calendar icon instead of a chevron.\n* **DatePicker.Portal**: Portal wrapper that re-provides DatePicker context across the portal boundary.\n* **DatePicker.Overlay**: Backdrop overlay behind the calendar content.\n* **DatePicker.Content**: Content container for the calendar popup. Supports `\"popover\"`, `\"dialog\"`, and `\"bottom-sheet\"` presentations.\n* **DatePicker.Calendar**: Pre-wired Calendar root that commits the selected date, updates the trigger label, and closes the overlay on selection. Uses `Calendar` compound parts as children.\n\n## Usage\n\n### Basic Usage\n\nThe DatePicker uses a popover presentation by default. Pass `Calendar` compound parts as children of `DatePicker.Calendar`.\n\n```tsx\n<DatePicker>\n  <Label>Event date</Label>\n  <DatePicker.Select>\n    <DatePicker.Trigger>\n      <DatePicker.Value />\n      <DatePicker.TriggerIndicator />\n    </DatePicker.Trigger>\n    <DatePicker.Portal>\n      <DatePicker.Overlay />\n      <DatePicker.Content presentation=\"popover\" width=\"trigger\">\n        <DatePicker.Calendar>\n          <Calendar.Header>\n            <Calendar.Heading />\n            <Calendar.NavButton slot=\"previous\" />\n            <Calendar.NavButton slot=\"next\" />\n          </Calendar.Header>\n          <Calendar.Grid>\n            <Calendar.GridHeader>\n              {(day) => <Calendar.HeaderCell day={day} />}\n            </Calendar.GridHeader>\n            <Calendar.GridBody>\n              {(date) => <Calendar.Cell date={date} />}\n            </Calendar.GridBody>\n          </Calendar.Grid>\n        </DatePicker.Calendar>\n      </DatePicker.Content>\n    </DatePicker.Portal>\n  </DatePicker.Select>\n</DatePicker>\n\n```\n\n### Controlled\n\nControl the selected date externally with `value` and `onValueChange`. The option stores an ISO date string in `value` and a display label shown in the trigger. You can also control the calendar overlay with `isOpen` and `onOpenChange`.\n\n```tsx\nimport type { DatePickerOption } from 'heroui-native-pro';\nimport { useState } from 'react';\n\nconst [selected, setSelected] = useState<DatePickerOption | undefined>({\n  value: '2026-06-15',\n  label: 'Jun 15, 2026',\n});\nconst [isOpen, setIsOpen] = useState(false);\n\n<DatePicker\n  value={selected}\n  onValueChange={setSelected}\n  isOpen={isOpen}\n  onOpenChange={setIsOpen}\n>\n  <Label>Event date</Label>\n  <DatePicker.Select>\n    <DatePicker.Trigger>\n      <DatePicker.Value />\n      <DatePicker.TriggerIndicator />\n    </DatePicker.Trigger>\n    <DatePicker.Portal>\n      <DatePicker.Overlay />\n      <DatePicker.Content presentation=\"popover\" width=\"trigger\">\n        <DatePicker.Calendar>\n          <Calendar.Header>\n            <Calendar.Heading />\n            <Calendar.NavButton slot=\"previous\" />\n            <Calendar.NavButton slot=\"next\" />\n          </Calendar.Header>\n          <Calendar.Grid>\n            <Calendar.GridHeader>\n              {(day) => <Calendar.HeaderCell day={day} />}\n            </Calendar.GridHeader>\n            <Calendar.GridBody>\n              {(date) => <Calendar.Cell date={date} />}\n            </Calendar.GridBody>\n          </Calendar.Grid>\n        </DatePicker.Calendar>\n      </DatePicker.Content>\n    </DatePicker.Portal>\n  </DatePicker.Select>\n</DatePicker>;\n\n```\n\n### Dialog Presentation\n\nDisplay the calendar in a centered modal dialog.\n\n```tsx\n<DatePicker>\n  <Label>Event date</Label>\n  <DatePicker.Select presentation=\"dialog\">\n    <DatePicker.Trigger>\n      <DatePicker.Value />\n      <DatePicker.TriggerIndicator />\n    </DatePicker.Trigger>\n    <DatePicker.Portal>\n      <DatePicker.Overlay />\n      <DatePicker.Content presentation=\"dialog\">\n        <DatePicker.Calendar>...</DatePicker.Calendar>\n      </DatePicker.Content>\n    </DatePicker.Portal>\n  </DatePicker.Select>\n</DatePicker>\n\n```\n\n### Bottom Sheet Presentation\n\nDisplay the calendar in a bottom sheet.\n\n```tsx\n<DatePicker>\n  <Label>Event date</Label>\n  <DatePicker.Select presentation=\"bottom-sheet\">\n    <DatePicker.Trigger>\n      <DatePicker.Value />\n      <DatePicker.TriggerIndicator />\n    </DatePicker.Trigger>\n    <DatePicker.Portal>\n      <DatePicker.Overlay />\n      <DatePicker.Content presentation=\"bottom-sheet\">\n        <DatePicker.Calendar>...</DatePicker.Calendar>\n      </DatePicker.Content>\n    </DatePicker.Portal>\n  </DatePicker.Select>\n</DatePicker>\n\n```\n\n### Display Format\n\nConfigure how the selected date is displayed in the trigger using `dateDisplayFormat`.\n\n```tsx\n<DatePicker dateDisplayFormat=\"short\">\n  <Label>Short format</Label>\n  <DatePicker.Select>\n    <DatePicker.Trigger>\n      <DatePicker.Value />\n      <DatePicker.TriggerIndicator />\n    </DatePicker.Trigger>\n    <DatePicker.Portal>\n      <DatePicker.Overlay />\n      <DatePicker.Content presentation=\"popover\" width=\"trigger\">\n        <DatePicker.Calendar>...</DatePicker.Calendar>\n      </DatePicker.Content>\n    </DatePicker.Portal>\n  </DatePicker.Select>\n</DatePicker>\n\n```\n\n### Custom Format Function\n\nOverride the display label entirely with `formatDate`.\n\n```tsx\nfunction formatSpanishDate(date: CalendarDate): string {\n  return new Intl.DateTimeFormat('es-ES', {\n    weekday: 'long',\n    day: 'numeric',\n    month: 'long',\n    year: 'numeric',\n  }).format(date.toDate(getLocalTimeZone()));\n}\n\n<DatePicker formatDate={formatSpanishDate} locale=\"es-ES\">\n  <Label>Custom format</Label>\n  <DatePicker.Select>\n    <DatePicker.Trigger>\n      <DatePicker.Value />\n      <DatePicker.TriggerIndicator />\n    </DatePicker.Trigger>\n    <DatePicker.Portal>\n      <DatePicker.Overlay />\n      <DatePicker.Content presentation=\"popover\" width=\"trigger\">\n        <DatePicker.Calendar>...</DatePicker.Calendar>\n      </DatePicker.Content>\n    </DatePicker.Portal>\n  </DatePicker.Select>\n</DatePicker>;\n\n```\n\n### Field States\n\nUse root props for required, invalid, and disabled states.\n\n```tsx\n<DatePicker isRequired>\n  <Label>Deadline</Label>\n  <DatePicker.Select>\n    <DatePicker.Trigger>\n      <DatePicker.Value placeholder=\"Select a deadline\" />\n      <DatePicker.TriggerIndicator />\n    </DatePicker.Trigger>\n    <DatePicker.Portal>\n      <DatePicker.Overlay />\n      <DatePicker.Content presentation=\"popover\" width=\"trigger\">\n        <DatePicker.Calendar>...</DatePicker.Calendar>\n      </DatePicker.Content>\n    </DatePicker.Portal>\n  </DatePicker.Select>\n  <Description>Required for the project timeline.</Description>\n</DatePicker>\n\n```\n\n### Invalid State with FieldError\n\nCombine `isInvalid` with FieldError to display validation messages. The trigger shows a danger border.\n\n```tsx\n<DatePicker isInvalid>\n  <Label>Ship date</Label>\n  <DatePicker.Select>\n    <DatePicker.Trigger>\n      <DatePicker.Value />\n      <DatePicker.TriggerIndicator />\n    </DatePicker.Trigger>\n    <DatePicker.Portal>\n      <DatePicker.Overlay />\n      <DatePicker.Content presentation=\"popover\" width=\"trigger\">\n        <DatePicker.Calendar>...</DatePicker.Calendar>\n      </DatePicker.Content>\n    </DatePicker.Portal>\n  </DatePicker.Select>\n  <Description hideOnInvalid>Must be a business day.</Description>\n  <FieldError>Please select a valid ship date.</FieldError>\n</DatePicker>\n\n```\n\n### With Year Picker\n\nUse `Calendar.YearPickerTrigger` inside `DatePicker.Calendar` to add a year picker overlay.\n\n```tsx\n<DatePicker.Calendar>\n  <Calendar.Header>\n    <Calendar.YearPickerTrigger>\n      <Calendar.YearPickerTriggerHeading />\n      <Calendar.YearPickerTriggerIndicator />\n    </Calendar.YearPickerTrigger>\n   
… (truncated)

{"response":"# empty-state Documentation (heroui-native-pro)\n\n---\n\n<page url=\"/native/components/empty-state\">\n# EmptyState\n\n**Category**: native\n**URL**: https://docs.heroui.pro/native/components/empty-state\n**Source**: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/heroui-inc/heroui/refs/heads/main/apps/docs/content/docs/native/components/(data-display)/empty-state.mdx\n> A placeholder for empty views with icon, title, description, and call-to-action to guide users.\n\n\n## Import\n\n```tsx\nimport { EmptyState } from 'heroui-native-pro';\n\n```\n\n## Anatomy\n\n```tsx\n<EmptyState>\n  <EmptyState.Header>\n    <EmptyState.Media>...</EmptyState.Media>\n    <EmptyState.Title>...</EmptyState.Title>\n    <EmptyState.Description>...</EmptyState.Description>\n  </EmptyState.Header>\n  <EmptyState.Content>...</EmptyState.Content>\n</EmptyState>\n\n```\n\n* **EmptyState**: Root container. Centers compound parts vertically with consistent spacing and padding, and cascades `disable-all` to animated descendants. Sub-components are fully optional.\n* **EmptyState.Header**: Groups `Media`, `Title`, and `Description` in a centered column.\n* **EmptyState.Media**: Optional container for an icon, avatar, or custom media block. Supports `variant=\"default\"` (render as-is) and `variant=\"icon\"` (circular muted surface).\n* **EmptyState.Title**: Primary heading text rendered with `accessibilityRole=\"header\"`.\n* **EmptyState.Description**: Secondary muted text below the title.\n* **EmptyState.Content**: Optional action area below the header for buttons, inputs, or other controls.\n\n## Usage\n\n### Basic usage\n\nCompose a header with title and description for a minimal empty state.\n\n```tsx\n<EmptyState>\n  <EmptyState.Header>\n    <EmptyState.Title>Inbox zero</EmptyState.Title>\n    <EmptyState.Description>...</EmptyState.Description>\n  </EmptyState.Header>\n</EmptyState>\n\n```\n\n### With icon media\n\nUse `variant=\"icon\"` to wrap the media in a circular muted surface.\n\n```tsx\n<EmptyState>\n  <EmptyState.Header>\n    <EmptyState.Media variant=\"icon\">\n      <BellIcon size={20} />\n    </EmptyState.Media>\n    <EmptyState.Title>No notifications yet</EmptyState.Title>\n    <EmptyState.Description>...</EmptyState.Description>\n  </EmptyState.Header>\n</EmptyState>\n\n```\n\n### With actions\n\nAdd an action area below the header with `EmptyState.Content`.\n\n```tsx\n<EmptyState>\n  <EmptyState.Header>\n    <EmptyState.Title>No matches found</EmptyState.Title>\n    <EmptyState.Description>...</EmptyState.Description>\n  </EmptyState.Header>\n  <EmptyState.Content>\n    <Button variant=\"outline\">Clear search</Button>\n  </EmptyState.Content>\n</EmptyState>\n\n```\n\n### With custom media\n\nUse `variant=\"default\"` (the default) to render media content as-is, such as an avatar or stacked avatars.\n\n```tsx\n<EmptyState>\n  <EmptyState.Header>\n    <EmptyState.Media>\n      <Avatar>...</Avatar>\n    </EmptyState.Media>\n    <EmptyState.Title>User is offline</EmptyState.Title>\n    <EmptyState.Description>...</EmptyState.Description>\n  </EmptyState.Header>\n  <EmptyState.Content>\n    <Button variant=\"secondary\">Leave message</Button>\n  </EmptyState.Content>\n</EmptyState>\n\n```\n\n### Bordered container\n\nApply a dashed border on the root to render an outlined empty state.\n\n```tsx\n<EmptyState className=\"rounded-2xl border border-dashed border-border\">\n  <EmptyState.Header>\n    <EmptyState.Media variant=\"icon\">\n      <RocketIcon size={20} />\n    </EmptyState.Media>\n    <EmptyState.Title>Start your first automation</EmptyState.Title>\n    <EmptyState.Description>...</EmptyState.Description>\n  </EmptyState.Header>\n  <EmptyState.Content>\n    <Button size=\"sm\" variant=\"outline\">\n      Get started\n    </Button>\n  </EmptyState.Content>\n</EmptyState>\n\n```\n\n## Example\n\n```tsx\nimport { Button } from 'heroui-native';\nimport { EmptyState } from 'heroui-native-pro';\nimport { View } from 'react-native';\nimport { BellIcon } from './icons/bell';\n\nexport default function EmptyStateExample() {\n  return (\n    <View className=\"flex-1 items-center justify-center px-5\">\n      <EmptyState>\n        <EmptyState.Header>\n          <EmptyState.Media variant=\"icon\">\n            <BellIcon size={20} colorClassName=\"accent-default-foreground\" />\n          </EmptyState.Media>\n          <EmptyState.Title>No notifications yet</EmptyState.Title>\n          <EmptyState.Description>\n            Stay in the loop by enabling push alerts for account activity and\n            reminders.\n          </EmptyState.Description>\n        </EmptyState.Header>\n        <EmptyState.Content className=\"w-full gap-2.5\">\n          <Button className=\"w-full max-w-xs\">Enable notifications</Button>\n          <Button className=\"w-full max-w-xs\" variant=\"outline\">\n            Settings\n          </Button>\n        </EmptyState.Content>\n      </EmptyState>\n    </View>\n  );\n}\n\n```\n\n## API Reference\n\n### EmptyState\n\n| prop           | type                      | default | description                                                                         |\n| -------------- | ------------------------- | ------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |\n| `children`     | `React.ReactNode`         | -       | Compound parts rendered inside the empty state container                            |\n| `className`    | `string`                  | -       | Additional CSS classes for the root container                                       |\n| `animation`    | `EmptyStateRootAnimation` | -       | Animation configuration for the empty state root (cascades to animated descendants) |\n| `...ViewProps` | `ViewProps`               | -       | All standard React Native View props are supported                                  |\n\n#### EmptyStateRootAnimation\n\nAnimation configuration for the EmptyState root. Can be:\n\n* `\"disable-all\"`: Disable all animations including children (cascades down through `AnimationSettingsProvider`)\n* `undefined`: Use default animations\n\n### EmptyState.Header\n\n| prop           | type              | default | description                                        |\n| -------------- | ----------------- | ------- | -------------------------------------------------- |\n| `children`     | `React.ReactNode` | -       | Header content (media, title, description)         |\n| `className`    | `string`          | -       | Additional CSS classes for the header wrapper      |\n| `...ViewProps` | `ViewProps`       | -       | All standard React Native View props are supported |\n\n### EmptyState.Media\n\n| prop           | type                     | default     | description                                        |\n| -------------- | ------------------------ | ----------- | -------------------------------------------------- |\n| `children`     | `React.ReactNode`        | -           | Media content to render (icon, avatar, etc.)       |\n| `variant`      | `EmptyStateMediaVariant` | `'default'` | Media visual treatment                             |\n| `className`    | `string`                 | -           | Additional CSS classes for the media container     |\n| `...ViewProps` | `ViewProps`              | -           | All standard React Native View props are supported |\n\n#### EmptyStateMediaVariant\n\n| type                  | description                                                                |\n| --------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------- |\n| `'default' \\| 'icon'` | `default` renders media as-is. `icon` wraps it in a circular muted surface |\n\n### EmptyState.Title\n\n| prop           | type              | default | description                                        |\n| -------------- | ----------------- | ------- | -------------------------------------------------- |\n| `children`     | `React.ReactNode` | -       | Title text content                                 |\n| `className`    | `string`          | -       | Additional CSS classes for the title text          |\n| `...TextProps` | `TextProps`       | -       | All standard React Native Text props are supported |\n\n### EmptyState.Description\n\n| prop           | type              | default | description                                        |\n| -------------- | ----------------- | ------- | -------------------------------------------------- |\n| `children`     | `React.ReactNode` | -       | Description text content                           |\n| `className`    | `string`          | -       | Additional CSS classes for the description text    |\n| `...TextProps` | `TextProps`       | -       | All standard React Native Text props are supported |\n\n### EmptyState.Content\n\n| prop           | type              | default | description                                        |\n| -------------- | ----------------- | ------- | -------------------------------------------------- |\n| `children`     | `React.ReactNode` | -       | Action content rendered below the header           |\n| `className`    | `string`          | -       | Additional CSS classes for the action container    |\n| `...ViewProps` | `ViewProps`       | -       | All standard React Native View props are supported |\n\n</page>\n\n---\n\n# bottom-sheet Documentation (heroui-native)\n\n**URL:** https://heroui.com/docs/native/components/bottom-sheet.mdx\n\n---\n\n<page url=\"/en/docs/native/components/bottom-sheet\">\n# BottomSheet\n\n**Category**: native\n**URL**: https://www.heroui.com/en/docs/native/components/bottom-sheet\n**Source**: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/heroui-inc/heroui/refs/heads/v3/apps/docs/content/docs/en/native/components/(overlays)/bottom-sheet.mdx\n> Displays a bottom sheet that slides up from the bottom with animated transitions and swipe-to-dismiss gestures.\n\n\n\n\n## Import\n\n```tsx\nimport { BottomSheet } from '

Thought complete
I'm realizing that FlashList isn't actually available in the mobile app's dependencies, so using the built-in FlatList would be safer for the stub since Metro can resolve it without needing extra packages. But the task specifically asks for FlashList with the Card components and pull-to-refresh functionality, which creates a conflict. Since this is a template that consumers will use to generate their own screens, I'll stick with FlashList as specified — developers can add the dependency when they first use the generated code, or the CLI can prompt them to install it. For the Icon component, heroui-native-pro doesn't actually export a dedicated Icon primitive, so I'll use Ionicons from `@expo/vector-icons` instead, which is the most portable approach for the icon references in the empty state and other UI elements. I'm structuring the icon set as a typed registry where semantic kebab-case keys map to Ionicons names with sensible defaults for size and accessibility labels. This gives consumers a clean way to reference icons throughout their feature without hardcoding icon names everywhere. Now I'm ready to outline the seven stubs I need to create, starting with the list component. I'm debating whether the provider stub should emit just the provider file or include the context and hook all in one file. The workspace convention splits them into separate files, but that means a single stub would be incomplete. I could either go with a self-contained provider.tsx that includes all three exports and note in the docblock that it should be split later, or stick to the convention and acknowledge that the stub only handles one piece of the triple. I'm leaning toward the all-in-one approach for now since it gives consumers a working template immediately, and they can refactor it later to match code-standards.md. Now I'm moving on to define the remaining stubs: a DI module using the `@stackra/container` pattern with decorators and a `forRoot` method, and an icon set stub that maps kebab-case keys to Ionicons components for HeroUI Native slots. I'm establishing the token markers that will be substituted across all stubs — things like `kebabResource`, `pascalResource`, `camelResource`, and various naming conventions for interfaces, modules, and i18n keys. Every file needs proper JSDoc headers, interfaces prefixed with `I`, and consistent file suffixes based on their type. I'm also checking that the interface naming follows the `I<PascalName>` pattern from the standards, and making sure all styling uses only Uniwind classes. Now I'm ready to start writing the stubs themselves. {"response":"# text Documentation (heroui-native)\n\n**URL:** https://heroui.com/docs/native/components/text.mdx\n\n---\n\n<page url=\"/en/docs/native/components/text\">\n# Typography\n\n**Category**: native\n**URL**: https://www.heroui.com/en/docs/native/components/text\n**Source**: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/heroui-inc/heroui/refs/heads/v3/apps/docs/content/docs/en/native/components/(typography)/text.mdx\n> Primitive typography component for rendering styled text with semantic type variants.\n\n\n\n\n## Import\n\n```tsx\nimport { Typography } from 'heroui-native';\n\n```\n\n## Anatomy\n\n```tsx\n<Typography>...</Typography>\n\n{/* Sub-components */}\n<Typography.Heading>...</Typography.Heading>\n<Typography.Paragraph>...</Typography.Paragraph>\n<Typography.Code>...</Typography.Code>\n\n```\n\n* **Typography**: Root text element. Selects a typography preset via `type` and exposes orthogonal `align`, `color`, `weight`, and `truncate` props.\n* **Typography.Heading**: Convenience wrapper restricted to heading types (`h1`–`h6`). Adds `accessibilityRole=\"header\"` automatically.\n* **Typography.Paragraph**: Convenience wrapper restricted to body types (`body`, `body-sm`, `body-xs`).\n* **Typography.Code**: Chip-styled inline monospaced text. Uses a platform-appropriate monospace font family.\n\n## Usage\n\n### Basic Usage\n\nThe Typography component renders body text by default.\n\n```tsx\n<Typography>Hello, world!</Typography>\n\n```\n\n### Type Variants\n\nUse the `type` prop to select a semantic typography preset.\n\n```tsx\n<Typography type=\"h1\">Heading 1</Typography>\n<Typography type=\"h2\">Heading 2</Typography>\n<Typography type=\"h3\">Heading 3</Typography>\n<Typography type=\"h4\">Heading 4</Typography>\n<Typography type=\"h5\">Heading 5</Typography>\n<Typography type=\"h6\">Heading 6</Typography>\n<Typography type=\"body\">Body text</Typography>\n<Typography type=\"body-sm\">Small body text</Typography>\n<Typography type=\"body-xs\">Extra-small body text</Typography>\n<Typography type=\"code\">Code snippet</Typography>\n\n```\n\n### Headings\n\nUse `Typography.Heading` for heading text with automatic header accessibility role.\n\n```tsx\n<Typography.Heading type=\"h1\">Page Title</Typography.Heading>\n<Typography.Heading type=\"h2\">Section Title</Typography.Heading>\n<Typography.Heading type=\"h3\">Subsection Title</Typography.Heading>\n\n```\n\n### Paragraphs\n\nUse `Typography.Paragraph` for body text.\n\n```tsx\n<Typography.Paragraph>\n  This is a paragraph of body text with the default size.\n</Typography.Paragraph>\n<Typography.Paragraph type=\"body-sm\">\n  This is smaller body text.\n</Typography.Paragraph>\n\n```\n\n### Code\n\nUse `Typography.Code` (or equivalently `<Typography type=\"code\">`) for inline code snippets. Both render a chip-styled, monospaced inline element with a subtle background, rounded corners, and a `self-start` layout so it does not stretch in flex containers. The platform monospace `fontFamily` is applied at the `Typography` root, so the two forms are interchangeable.\n\n```tsx\n<Typography.Code>console.log('hello')</Typography.Code>\n<Typography type=\"code\">console.log('hello')</Typography>\n\n```\n\n### Alignment\n\nUse the `align` prop to control horizontal alignment. `start` and `end` are RTL-aware (they flip under right-to-left layouts).\n\n```tsx\n<Typography align=\"start\">Start-aligned</Typography>\n<Typography align=\"center\">Center-aligned</Typography>\n<Typography align=\"end\">End-aligned</Typography>\n<Typography align=\"justify\">Justified text spreads across the line.</Typography>\n\n```\n\n> **Note:** `text-justify` is iOS-only on React Native; Android falls back to left alignment.\n\n### Color\n\nUse the `color` prop to apply a semantic foreground color preset.\n\n```tsx\n<Typography color=\"default\">Default foreground</Typography>\n<Typography color=\"muted\">Muted secondary text</Typography>\n\n```\n\nFor other theme colors, pass the corresponding utility through `className` (e.g. `className=\"text-accent\"`, `className=\"text-danger\"`).\n\n### Weight\n\nUse the `weight` prop to override the font weight implied by `type`. The override merges via `tailwind-merge`, so it always wins over the type variant's default weight.\n\n```tsx\n<Typography type=\"h1\" weight=\"bold\">Bold H1</Typography>\n<Typography weight=\"medium\">Medium body</Typography>\n<Typography weight=\"semibold\">Semibold body</Typography>\n\n```\n\n### Truncation\n\nUse the `truncate` boolean prop to limit the text to a single line with an ellipsis. It is mapped to React Native's `numberOfLines={1}`. An explicit `numberOfLines` prop, if provided, takes precedence.\n\n```tsx\n<Typography truncate>\n  A long line of text that will be cut off with an ellipsis when it overflows\n  the container.\n</Typography>;\n\n{\n  /* Multi-line truncation via the underlying RN prop */\n}\n<Typography numberOfLines={2}>\n  Two-line truncation works through React Native's standard `numberOfLines`\n  prop.\n</Typography>;\n\n```\n\n## Example\n\n```tsx\nimport { Typography } from 'heroui-native';\nimport { View } from 'react-native';\n\nexport default function TypographyExample() {\n  return (\n    <View className=\"flex-1 justify-center px-5 gap-4\">\n      <Typography.Heading type=\"h1\">Welcome</Typography.Heading>\n      <Typography.Heading type=\"h3\">Getting Started</Typography.Heading>\n      <Typography.Paragraph>\n        This is a body paragraph rendered with the Typography component.\n      </Typography.Paragraph>\n      <Typography.Paragraph color=\"muted\" type=\"body-sm\">\n        Smaller supporting text for captions or footnotes.\n      </Typography.Paragraph>\n      <Typography.Code>npm install heroui-native</Typography.Code>\n    </View>\n  );\n}\n\n```\n\nYou can find more examples in the [GitHub repository](https://github.com/heroui-inc/heroui-native/blob/main/example/src/app/\\(home\\)/components/text.tsx).\n\n## API Reference\n\n### Typography\n\n`Typography` extends all standard React Native `TextProps` with additional typography props.\n\n| prop           | type                                                                                         | default     | description                                                                                                                    |\n| -------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |\n| `type`         | `'h1' \\| 'h2' \\| 'h3' \\| 'h4' \\| 'h5' \\| 'h6' \\| 'body' \\| 'body-sm' \\| 'body-xs' \\| 'code'` | `'body'`    | Semantic typography variant (size, default weight, line-height)                                                                |\n| `align`        | `'start' \\| 'center' \\| 'end' \\| 'justify'`                                                  | `'start'`   | Horizontal alignment. `start` and `end` are RTL-aware. `justify` is iOS-only.                                                  |\n| `color`        | `'default' \\| 'muted'`                                                                       | `'default'` | Semantic foreground color preset                                                                                               |\n| `weight`       | `'normal' \\| 'medium' \\| 'semibold' \\| 'bold'`                                               | -           | Font weight override. When set, overrides the weight implied by `type`.                                                        |\n| `truncate`     | `boolean`                                                                                    | `false`     | Truncates the text to a single line with an ellipsis (sets `numberOfLines={1}`). An explicit `numberOfLines` takes precedence. |\n| `children`     | `React.ReactNode`                                                                            | -           | Content to render                                                                                                              |\n| `className`    | `string`                                                                                     | -           | Additional CSS classes                                                                                                         |\n| `...TextProps` | `TextProps`                                                                                  | -           | All standard React Native `Text` props are supported                                                                           |\n\n### Typography.Heading\n\nInherits all `Typography` root props (`align`, `color`, `weight`, `truncate`, `className`, and React Native `TextProps`). Sets `accessibilityRole=\"header\"` automatically and narrows `type` to heading variants.\n\n| prop           | type                                           | default | description                                          |\n| -------------- | ---------------------------------------------- | ------- | ---------------------------------------------------- |\n| `type`         | `'h1' \\| 'h2' \\| 'h3' \\| 'h4' \\| 'h5' \\| 'h6'` | `'h1'`  | Heading level                                        |\n| `children`     | `React.ReactNode`                              | -       | Content to render                                    |\n| `className`    | `string`                                       | -       | Additional CSS classes                               |\n| `...TextProps` | `TextProps`                                    | -       | All standard React Native `Text` props are supported |\n\n### Typography.Paragraph\n\nInherits all `Typography` root props (`align`, `color`, `weight`, `truncate`, `className`, and React Native `TextProps`). Narrows `type` to body variants.\n\n| prop           | type                               | default

I'm including tokens for PagerDuty integration, dashboard links, and escalation paths, along with sections for likely causes, mitigations, and post-incident procedures. For release notes, I'm defining the structure with version, date, and a TL;DR summary, then breaking down new features, breaking changes with migration guidance, fixes, known issues, and upgrade instructions. For the entity relationship diagram stub, I'm setting up tokens for feature name and date, then planning sections for aggregate definitions, per-entity column specifications, trait composition, and data retention windows. For the threat model, I'm organizing sections around system context, data flow diagrams, trust boundaries, STRIDE analysis per component, attack trees, and a Phase-5 verification checklist. For architecture, I'm structuring it with context and goals, module surfaces, service boundaries, transport selection, tenancy patterns, entitlement gates, sequence diagrams, ADR references, and open questions. Now I'm matching the ADR stub's formatting pattern with headers, metadata bullets, sections, subsections, and HTML comment footers. Filling in the module ownership table with the three modules and their respective owners, new surface areas, and consumed dependencies, then mapping out which service owns which responsibility across identity, commerce, backend, AI, and observability layers. Finally, I'm documenting the transport choices for each interaction—REST, events, RPC, or local calls—with the rationale behind each decision. Generating markdown table... for f in $(find backend-packages -maxdepth 3 -name composer.json -type f | sort); do
  echo "=====$f====="
  cat "$f" | python3 -c "import sys,json; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print('NAME:', d.get('name','')); print('DESC:', d.get('description','')); reqs=d.get('require', {}); acad=[k for k in reqs if k.startswith('stackra/') or k.startswith('stackra-')]; print('PEER:', ','.join(sorted(acad)))"
done 2>&1 | head -400

Thought complete

1 tool call
Command
/Users/akouta/Projects/stackra-frontend
python3 -c "
import json, glob, pathlib
paths = sorted(glob.glob('backend-packages/*/composer.json') + glob.glob('backend-packages/*/*/composer.json'))
for p in paths:
    d = json.load(open(p))
    name = d.get('name','')
    desc = d.get('description','')
    reqs = d.get('require', {})
    peers = sorted(k for k in reqs if k.startswith('stackra/') or k.startswith('stackra-'))
    print(f'PATH:{p}')
    print(f'NAME:{name}')
    print(f'DESC:{desc}')
    print(f'PEER:{\",\".join(peers)}')
    print('---')
" 2>&1 | head -600

Thought complete
I've confirmed there are no existing catalog.json files in any of the 26 target directories, so I'm ready to generate them. I need to be precise with the formatting: keeping purposes under 200 characters as single sentences with periods, selecting 3-8 concise capability phrases, writing single-sentence descriptions for both when to use and when not to use cases (with the latter referencing sibling packages), and noting that peer dependencies are empty. For the actual paths, I'm clarifying that `foundation/foundation` and `authorization/authorization` from the tier documentation map to the on-disk roots at `backend-packages/foundation/` and `backend-packages/authorization/`, where I'll place each catalog.json and reference the corresponding README files. All 26 packages will share common fields: tier set to `foundation` for the 2 kernel packages and `framework` for the other 24, surfaces as `["core"]`, kind as `framework-plumbing`, backend_pair as null, empty size_gzip_kb object, maturity as `alpha`, and owning_agent as `"laravel-feature-builder"`. For peer dependencies, I'll extract the `stackra/*` packages (excluding illuminate/* and dev-only ones) plus any heavily-used external composer packages that define the runtime behavior, like spatie packages and laravel/pennant. Writing each catalog.json now... For foundation, the description captures that it's the shared kernel every Stackra package depends on, providing the module service-provider base class, cross-cutting contracts like HasErrorCode and Correlatable, correlation-id middleware, guard-clause helpers, an AppEnvironment enum, and a testable SystemClock. The capabilities list those core features, when_to_use explains it's mandatory for any Stackra Laravel package, and when_not_to_use clarifies that for standalone utility helpers without Laravel wiring, you'd reach for stackra/support instead. Peer dependencies include stackra/enum... Now looking at the Foundation module itself — this is the blueprint-generated server-side package that provides the shared Foundation surface (Data DTOs, actions, repositories) auto-regenerated from the module blueprint. It works alongside the foundation-sdk wire package and depends on the kernel plus routing, CRUD, and Spatie's laravel-data for attribute-first models. When to use it is when an app needs that server surface from the blueprint, and when not to use it is when you're consuming the wire SDK across service boundaries instead. The authorization package is an attribute-driven controller layer that gates endpoints with #[RequirePermission], #[RequireRole], and #[AllowGuest] attributes, all reflection-cached and backend-agnostic with no coupling to permission tables. It fails 401/403 before the controller runs and has zero models or migrations. Use it for simple attribute-based endpoint gating, and reach for the heavier access package if you need persisted roles and admin CRUD. The architecture package enforces layering rules across the monorepo by scanning source files and preventing direct model access outside repositories, factories, or seeders via a rule engine and artisan command. It includes marker attributes, a PHPStan extension, and optional enforcement that controllers can't bypass services to reach repositories. Use it at CI time to enforce layered architecture ADRs; for static typing alone, PHPStan is sufficient. Now I'm cataloging the caching package, which is an attribute-driven cache composition system with decorators for cacheable methods, eviction, put operations, keys, tags, TTL, and custom tag resolvers. Wrapping up the console package description at around 172 characters, then moving through its capabilities like the extended command attribute, BaseCommand wiring, lifecycle hooks, and per-command extension discovery. The container package comes next — it's a thin wrapper around illuminate/container that primarily ships the #[Overrides] attribute for vendor-side binding replacements, along with some additional container helpers. For the container package, #[Overrides] lets you replace a third-party abstract when you can't annotate the interface directly with #[Bind]. Since container only depends on Laravel core, there are no stackra peer dependencies to list. Now I'm moving into the CRUD package, which is attribute-driven and includes decorators like #[AsRepository], #[UseModel], #[Filterable], and #[Sortable] for building out repository, service, and controller layers with a pre-resolved boot-time registry. I'm working through the capabilities list for the database package, trying to keep each item under 100 characters. I'm grouping the data-shaping attributes and model concerns into shorter, more digestible entries, and I need to finish describing the media interaction concern that got cut off. Now I'm moving on to the enum package — it's a modern PHP 8.1+ system with composable traits and reflection-cached metadata. The description is too long at 145 characters, so I need to condense it. The capabilities cover the trait composition, attribute declarations, reflection caching, and various helper methods for accessing labels and values. I'm defining when to use it (any backed enum needing metadata) and when not to use it (simple value unions without metadata). Next is the events package, which handles attribute-based listener discovery and broadcasting. The description is 174 characters, also over the limit. The capabilities include listener discovery attributes, transaction-safe listeners, broadcasting configuration, and compile-time discovery that eliminates manual event maps. I'm noting when to use it (domain events and listeners in packages) versus when not to (direct request/response calls). Moving on to the exceptions package, which provides a structured exception hierarchy with JSON rendering, Ignition integration, and Sentry enrichment. The description is 148 characters. Key capabilities are the base exception class with translation keys, RFC 7807-adjacent JSON envelopes, Ignition solutions, AI-augmented suggestions in dev mode, and Sentry context enrichment with redaction for sensitive data. This is used when packages raise domain exceptions to ensure uniform JSON formatting and tracing, but not for controller-side validation errors. Now looking at the feature-flags package entry. Listing the peer dependencies for the feature-flag package, then moving on to document the terminal UI toolkit — a full-featured Artisan command enhancement with live rendering, async orchestration, spinners, and HTML-to-ANSI conversion. Then starting to catalog the routing package with attribute-based routing support. peer dependencies are `stackra/foundation` and `stackra/service-provider`. Moving on to the scheduling package, which handles attribute-based task scheduling — I'm documenting how `#[Schedule]`, `#[Cron]`, and `#[WithoutOverlapping]` let you declare recurring tasks directly on Job and Command classes without touching the Kernel. Then there's the scope package starting to come into view. The serializer package wraps league/fractal for payload transformation across services, though it's fairly minimal and mainly serves legacy transformer paths — new endpoints should use spatie/laravel-data DTOs instead. Moving on to the service-provider package, which provides an attribute-driven base for module registration with declarative resource loading, cached discovery, and lifecycle hooks as a modern alternative to hand-rolled boot and register methods. Continuing with the field metadata, registry, and service layer, plus the audit trail and built-in system setting classes for auth, mail, notifications, and storage — consumers access these through the SettingsService. The package integrates with spatie/laravel-settings and supports scope-based namespace registration. I'd use this when adding tenant-configurable settings via attributes, but for theme-specific configuration I'd reach for the sibling theme package instead. Now looking at the support utilities package, which provides reflection helpers, string and array utilities, path handling, case conversion, email validation, and password tools — mostly zero-Laravel-runtime where possible. I'm noticing a mismatch between what the telemetry packages declare in their composer.json files and the actual package name in the support package — they reference `stackra/support` but the real composer name is `stackra/support`. Rather than try to fix this discrepancy, I'll record what's actually declared in the composer.json files and flag the inconsistency in my report, since that's the source of truth for the catalog. Now I'm working through the health package — it provides attribute-driven health check discovery that bridges Spatie's Laravel Health library with an `#[AsHealthCheck]` attribute, plus a notification channel enum for routing alerts to Slack, PagerDuty, or Email. The peer dependencies are `stackra/foundation` and `spatie/laravel-health`. Moving on to the Horizon package, which handles Laravel queue monitoring with built-in supervisor and metric discovery. Finishing up the peer dependencies for Nightwatch, then moving into Sentry's configuration — it tracks errors and exceptions with automatic context enrichment, distinguishing itself from Horizon's queue supervision and Nightwatch's structured observability. Now I'm ready to generate all 26 catalog files organized by tier, using repo-relative paths for documentation.

python3 - <<'PY'
import json, sys, glob

targets = [
    "backend-packages/foundation/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/shared/foundation/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/authorization/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/compliance/architecture/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/framework/caching/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/framework/console/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/framework/container/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/framework/crud/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/framework/database/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/framework/enum/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/framework/events/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/framework/exceptions/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/framework/feature-flags/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/framework/omniterm/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/framework/routing/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/framework/scheduling/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/framework/scope/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/framework/serializer/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/framework/service-provider/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/framework/settings/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/framework/support/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/telemetry/debug-bar/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/telemetry/health/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/telemetry/horizon/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/telemetry/nightwatch/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/telemetry/sentry/catalog.json",
]

schema = json.load(open(".ref/schemas/catalog.v1.json"))
required_root = schema["required"]
allowed_root = list(schema["properties"].keys())
allowed_surfaces = schema["properties"]["surfaces"]["items"]["enum"]
allowed_tier = schema["properties"]["tier"]["enum"]
allowed_kind = schema["properties"]["kind"]["enum"]
allowed_maturity = schema["properties"]["maturity"]["enum"]
purpose_max = schema["properties"]["purpose"]["maxLength"]
cap_max = schema["properties"]["capabilities"]["items"]["maxLength"]

problems = []
ok = 0
for p in targets:
    try:
        d = json.load(open(p))
    except Exception as e:
        problems.append(f"[{p}] JSON parse failed: {e}")
        continue
    # required
    for r in required_root:
        if r not in d:
            problems.append(f"[{p}] missing required field: {r}")
    # additionalProperties: false — check no stray keys
    stray = [k for k in d.keys() if k not in allowed_root]
    if stray:
        problems.append(f"[{p}] stray keys: {stray}")
    # enum fields
    if d.get("tier") not in allowed_tier:
        problems.append(f"[{p}] bad tier: {d.get('tier')}")
    if d.get("kind") and d.get("kind") not in allowed_kind:
        problems.append(f"[{p}] bad kind: {d.get('kind')}")
    if d.get("maturity") and d.get("maturity") not in allowed_maturity:
        problems.append(f"[{p}] bad maturity: {d.get('maturity')}")
    for s in d.get("surfaces", []):
        if s not in allowed_surfaces:
            problems.append(f"[{p}] bad surface: {s}")
    # length caps
    if d.get("purpose") and len(d["purpose"]) > purpose_max:
        problems.append(f"[{p}] purpose too long ({len(d['purpose'])} > {purpose_max})")
    for i, c in enumerate(d.get("capabilities", [])):
        if len(c) > cap_max:
            problems.append(f"[{p}] capability[{i}] too long ({len(c)} > {cap_max}): {c!r}")
    # name pattern
    import re
    if d.get("name"):
        if not re.match(r'^([a-z0-9-]+/[a-z0-9-]+|@[a-z0-9-]+/[a-z0-9-]+)$', d["name"]):
            problems.append(f"[{p}] name fails pattern: {d['name']}")
    ok += 1

print(f"Files inspected: {ok}/{len(targets)}")
if problems:
    print(f"\nProblems ({len(problems)}):")
    for pr in problems:
        print(f"  - {pr}")
    sys.exit(1)
else:
    print("All catalogs valid against the v1 schema.")
PY

/Users/akouta/Projects/stackra-frontend
python3 <<'PY'
import json, sys, re

targets = [
    "backend-packages/foundation/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/shared/foundation/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/authorization/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/compliance/architecture/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/framework/caching/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/framework/console/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/framework/container/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/framework/crud/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/framework/database/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/framework/enum/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/framework/events/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/framework/exceptions/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/framework/feature-flags/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/framework/omniterm/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/framework/routing/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/framework/scheduling/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/framework/scope/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/framework/serializer/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/framework/service-provider/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/framework/settings/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/framework/support/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/telemetry/debug-bar/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/telemetry/health/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/telemetry/horizon/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/telemetry/nightwatch/catalog.json",
    "backend-packages/telemetry/sentry/catalog.json",
]

schema = json.load(open(".ref/schemas/catalog.v1.json"))
required_root = schema["required"]
allowed_root = list(schema["properties"].keys())
allowed_surfaces = schema["properties"]["surfaces"]["items"]["enum"]
allowed_tier = schema["properties"]["tier"]["enum"]
allowed_kind = schema["properties"]["kind"]["enum"]
allowed_maturity = schema["properties"]["maturity"]["enum"]
purpose_max = schema["properties"]["purpose"]["maxLength"]
cap_max = schema["properties"]["capabilities"]["items"]["maxLength"]
name_re = re.compile(r'^([a-z0-9-]+/[a-z0-9-]+|@[a-z0-9-]+/[a-z0-9-]+)$')

problems = []
ok = 0
for p in targets:
    try:
        d = json.load(open(p))
    except Exception as e:
        problems.append(f"[{p}] JSON parse failed: {e}")
        continue
    for r in required_root:
        if r not in d:
            problems.append(f"[{p}] missing required: {r}")
    stray = [k for k in d.keys() if k not in allowed_root]
    if stray:
        problems.append(f"[{p}] stray keys: {stray}")
    if d.get("tier") not in allowed_tier:
        problems.append(f"[{p}] bad tier: {d.get('tier')}")
    if d.get("kind") and d.get("kind") not in allowed_kind:
        problems.append(f"[{p}] bad kind: {d.get('kind')}")
    if d.get("maturity") and d.get("maturity") not in allowed_maturity:
        problems.append(f"[{p}] bad maturity: {d.get('maturity')}")
    for s in d.get("surfaces", []):
        if s not in allowed_surfaces:
            problems.append(f"[{p}] bad surface: {s}")
    if d.get("purpose") and len(d["purpose"]) > purpose_max:
        problems.append(f"[{p}] purpose too long ({len(d['purpose'])} > {purpose_max})")
    for i, c in enumerate(d.get("capabilities", [])):
        if len(c) > cap_max:
            problems.append(f"[{p}] capability[{i}] too long ({len(c)} > {cap_max})")
    if d.get("name") and not name_re.match(d["name"]):
        problems.append(f"[{p}] name fails pattern: {d['name']}")
    ok += 1

print(f"Files inspected: {ok}/{len(targets)}")
if problems:
    print(f"\nProblems ({len(problems)}):")
    for pr in problems:
        print("  - " + pr)
    sys.exit(1)
print("All catalogs valid against the v1 schema.")

I've verified the format model and now I'm organizing the catalog structure across five access categories and billing, mapping each composer name to its corresponding stackra path before generating all 44 catalog.json files. Finishing the Shared section with offline-sync, search, telemetry, transfer, and versioning packages. That brings the total to 44 packages across all categories. I'm verifying that every package name matches the schema pattern — including the hyphenated vendor names like `stackra-observability/activity` which are valid. Now I'm moving into building the catalog.json entries, where I'll need to craft purposes under 200 characters, define capabilities, add usage guidance, and pull in peer dependencies from the Composer requirements. Writing catalog.json files... Writing purpose descriptions... , and refund via the gateway abstraction." → ~105 chars ✓ Continuing through the finance domain with payout, refund, tax, transaction, and wallet — each capturing a distinct piece of the money-movement lifecycle. Then moving into notifications with announcements, messaging, newsletter, and the universal notification substrate handling delivery records, templates, preferences, and digests. Now detailing the notification channels: in-app writes to the inbox and broadcasts over Reverb, mail uses Blade rendering with RFC headers and provider webhooks, push covers FCM/APNs/Expo/OneSignal with device-token management, and SMS routes through Twilio/MessageBird/Vonage with TCPA compliance and cost tracking. Shifting to observability — activity, audit, and monitoring modules all blueprint-emitted with schema adaptation and query surfaces on top of vendor substrates. Then shared utilities starting with a tenant-facing activity feed built on spatie/laravel-activitylog with tenant scoping, ULID prefixes, tier-based retention, and Sanctum causer resolution. Continuing through the shared layer: attributes stores extensible entity-attribute-value data, audit provides compliance-grade trails with KMS encryption and long-term retention, geography wraps a global reference catalog with countries/states/cities/currencies/languages/timezones and IP resolution, localization layers multilingual support with DB-backed translator caching and machine-translation drivers, offline-sync buffers client operations during disconnection and reconciles on reconnect, search is an enterprise engine with attribute-driven registration and faceted analytics, telemetry captures structured performance signals, transfer handles import/export with sharded queued execution, and versioning manages public interface evolution with SemVer/CalVer support. Now I'm generating the catalog.json files for all 44 features. Each follows a consistent structure with tier "saas", surfaces ["core"], kind "feature", maturity "alpha", owning_agent "laravel-feature-builder", and null backend_pair. I'll start systematically with the Access and Billing group.

const s = require('./.ref/schemas/catalog.v1.json');
const sample = {
  name: 'stackra/auth',
  tier: 'saas',
  surfaces: ['core'],
  kind: 'feature',
  purpose: 'Test.',
  capabilities: ['a','b','c'],
  when_to_use: 'W',
  when_not_to_use: 'W not.',
  peer_deps: ['stackra/foundation'],
  backend_pair: null,
  size_gzip_kb: {},
  maturity: 'alpha',
  owning_agent: 'laravel-feature-builder',
  docs: ['backend-packages/identity/auth/README.md']
};
// Basic schema check
const missing = s.required.filter(k => !(k in sample));
const extra = Object.keys(sample).filter(k => !(k in s.properties));
console.log('missing:', missing);
console.log('extra:', extra);
console.log('name pattern OK:', new RegExp(s.properties.name.pattern).test(sample.name));
"

Thought complete
I'm checking the name pattern requirements — it needs exactly two segments separated by a slash, like `scope/name` or `@scope/name`, so something like `stackra-platform/application-sdk` would actually match since both parts contain only lowercase letters, numbers, and hyphens. Now I'm mapping out the full package inventory across three domains. The Identity domain has seven packages like `stackra/auth` and `stackra/identity`, the Growth domain has five packages including `stackra/analytics` and `stackra/marketing`, and the Platform domain contains eighteen packages starting with `stackra/admin-console`. Finishing the SDK list with the remaining packages, then I need to carefully construct each catalog.json entry. For every package, I'm defining its purpose in under 200 characters, listing 3–8 capabilities drawn from the README, specifying when to use it and when not to use it (with a named alternative), and identifying the meaningful peer dependencies from composer.json while filtering out framework basics like illuminate and spatie packages, plus the foundational stackra modules that every package depends on. So the frontend samples include the universal dependencies in their peer_deps, which means I should do the same for backend packages. I'll extract the actual dependencies from each `composer.json`, filter out third-party vendors and PHP itself, but keep everything under the `stackra/*` and `stackra-*/*` namespaces. For SDK packages specifically, they follow a different pattern with `stackra/api-sdk`, `stackra/exceptions`, `stackra/foundation`, and `stackra/service-provider` as the core set. Now I'm ready to start authoring the files. I'll tackle the three bucket-level READMEs first since those need unique content, then work through the 38 catalog.json files systematically, starting with the Identity bucket and its seven packages. RecoveryCodesDownload and TotpEnrollmentPreview DTOs handle the MFA enrollment surface — TOTP two-round setup, WebAuthn credential storage, recovery code generation, and per-Identity MFA state tracking. This module manages MFA method enrollment and recovery, distinct from the auth service which handles MFA challenges during login flows. The people module then layers on top with global PersonIdentity records, cross-tenant guardian-minor relationships, and consent-gated tenant linking for central-plane identity resolution. ServiceAccount handles machine-to-machine authentication via Sanctum PATs with default-deny permissions, while the User module provides the per-application projection of an Identity with its PII Profile and TenantMember role binding to a specific tenant. Now I'm looking at the growth packages, starting with analytics which captures the canonical event stream, resolves identity across sessions, and manages per-tenant provider configurations with delivery logs for fan-out to external analytics platforms. Next is attribution, which maintains an append-only touchpoint ledger and rolling attribution profiles per subject, handling first-touch and last-touch resolution along with UTM and campaign parsing while respecting consent constraints. Then there's leads, which provides CRM primitives for prospective families including Lead records, follow-up tasks, and an activity timeline with lifecycle states and staff assignment workflows. Marketing handles the event stream for dispatching messages across configured providers, tracking delivery attempts, and maintaining a dead-letter queue for replays. Finally, referrals is starting to be defined with entities like ReferralCode and ReferralFraudFlag. ersonalizes per-user dashboard layouts. The admin-console module handles per-tenant and per-user configuration for dashboard widgets, layout persistence, and shortcuts — useful when rendering the Stackra admin interface but not for public-facing sites or tenant branding concerns. Moving on to the AI integration substrate, which manages conversation ledgers, tool-call audits, draft-then-confirm workflows for write operations, and vector embeddings for RAG systems. This tracks AI runs and maintains sensitivity boundaries around tool access, and it's the right fit when persisting conversations and embeddings but not for the AI service's internal logic like personas or sensitivity gates themselves. The Application module serves as the global product catalogue registry, holding the cross-tenant Application rows (Sports, Marketplace, etc.) and the dual-source BusinessType catalogue (academy, gym, school, etc.). It handles application resolution per tenant, BusinessType administration with custom overrides, and immutability guardrails on system rows — but it's not the place for per-tenant Tenant configuration, which belongs in the tenancy module. Finally, the Branding module provides white-label substrate with per-tenant theme customization, logo variants, typography, and CSS variables that resolve differently across domains and modes. "stackra/crud", "stackra/routing", "stackra/service-provider", "stackra/support", "stackra-platform/credentials-sdk"] Now mapping out the domains package — it handles custom domain registration per tenant, DNS verification workflows, and certificate provisioning through pluggable ACME providers. The forms package is next, managing form definitions with versioned snapshots and per-submission records. Then there's integrations, which stores encrypted third-party credentials scoped to each tenant — Stripe, Slack, OAuth tokens, and similar — with KMS-backed encryption and automatic token refresh cycles. Moving into organization, which models structural sub-brands within a tenant, supporting optional nesting for enterprise customers who need multiple branded divisions under one account. And public-site handles the tenant-facing website engine with reusable content blocks and pages for landing pages and contact forms. Now looking at realtime, which manages WebSocket channels, active subscriptions, and presence tracking for online status — handling broadcast dispatch and authorization callbacks for real-time messaging needs. Moving on to region, which aggregates commercial and regulatory zones with currency, tax rules, timezone, and locale defaults scoped per tenant — useful when modeling multi-region setups or resolving defaults for different geographic surfaces. Then there's reporting, which provides dashboards, report definitions, saved reports per user, and execution logs with materialized outputs — supporting async materialization and schedule/trigger primitives for tenant-facing analytics. For staff, I'm looking at the employment substrate wrapping a User with Coach as a satellite entity carrying per-sport coaching profiles, scoped to branches and composable via a BelongsToUser trait. Finally, storage handles tenant-scoped file uploads with polymorphic ownership, image and PDF variants, audited signed URLs, and chunked-upload state management. LogContext and CachePrefix tenancy hooks plus lifecycle events; used when scoping rows to a Tenant or resolving the active tenant from a request, but not for per-tenant User rows. Then there's the theme substrate with ThemePreset, ThemeTokenOverride, and Theme entities that layer sparse token overrides on top of preset libraries, supporting dark/light mode variants and auditable changes — useful when rendering tenant-selected themes or managing presets, though branding assets like logos stay in a separate module. The webhook substrate handles outbound subscriptions with HMAC-SHA256 signing and rotation grace periods, plus per-attempt delivery audit trails; it supports pluggable destinations like HTTPS, EventBridge, and PubSub, batch fan-out, auto-disable on repeated failures, per-subscription rate limiting, and API-version pinning — deployed when dispatching outbound events or managing tenant-defined subscriptions, but inbound ingestion and third-party credentials belong elsewhere. Now I'm moving into the SDK packages, starting with the Access SDK as a typed Saloon umbrella for roles, permissions, policies, and scopes across per-module resource siblings, using attribute-driven discovery and bearer auth — useful when a Laravel app needs HTTP calls to the Access service but not for direct database access to roles. The API SDK itself is the attribute-driven typed kernel powering Saloon v3 connectors with discovery machinery, typed exception hierarchy, retry logic, correlation IDs, and mock support. The Compliance SDK follows the same pattern as Billing, providing typed access to DSAR, consent, and retention workflows through HTTP. Then Identity SDK wraps the auth and session management layer with MFA, OAuth clients, and JWKS discovery using the same per-module resource composition approach. Notifications SDK extends this further for multi-channel messaging—email, SMS, push, in-app—all discoverable through the #[AsSdkResource] attribute and configured via sdk.notifications.* settings. Platform Application SDK appears to be the wire-visible surface layer for the broader platform. Use it when a Laravel app needs to call the Platform service over HTTP with typed responses, but skip it for direct database access to platform rows. The peer dependencies are foundation, api-sdk, exceptions, and service-provider. I'm verifying the package naming pattern and checking that the purpose strings stay under 200 characters — the longer descriptions are coming in around 150–185 chars, which fits the constraint. Now I'm ready to start authoring the files. I'll tackle the three bucket-level READMEs first, then batch out the 38 catalog.json files systematically.

2 tool calls
Command
/Users/akouta/Projects/stackra-frontend
node -e "
const fs = require('node:fs');
const path = require('node:path');
const s = JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync('.ref/schemas/catalog.v1.json','utf8'));
const props = s.properties;
const dirs = [
  'backend-packages/identity',
  'backend-packages/growth',
  'backend-packages/platform',
  'backend-packages/sdk'
];
const namePattern = new RegExp(props.name.pattern);
let ok = 0, bad = 0;
for (const d of dirs) {
  for (const e of fs.readdirSync(d, {withFileTypes:true})) {
    if (!e.isDirectory()) continue;
    const p = path.join(d, e.name, 'catalog.json');
    if (!fs.existsSync(p)) continue;
    let raw;
    try { raw = JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync(p,'utf8')); }
    catch (err) { console.log('PARSE-FAIL', p, err.message); bad++; continue; }
    const issues = [];
    for (const k of s.required) if (!(k in raw)) issues.push('missing:' + k);
    for (const k of Object.keys(raw)) if (!(k in props)) issues.push('extra:' + k);
    if (!namePattern.test(raw.name)) issues.push('name-pattern:' + raw.name);
    if (!props.tier.enum.includes(raw.tier)) issues.push('tier:' + raw.tier);
    if (!props.kind.enum.includes(raw.kind)) issues.push('kind:' + raw.kind);
    for (const sur of (raw.surfaces||[])) if (!props.surfaces.items.enum.includes(sur)) issues.push('surface:' + sur);
    if (raw.purpose && raw.purpose.length > props.purpose.maxLength) issues.push('purpose-len:' + raw.purpose.length);
    for (const c of (raw.capabilities||[])) if (c.length > props.capabilities.items.maxLength) issues.push('cap-len:' + c.length);
    if (!props.maturity.enum.includes(raw.maturity)) issues.push('maturity:' + raw.maturity);
    if (issues.length) { console.log('FAIL', p, issues); bad++; }
    else ok++;
  }
}
console.log(\`\\n${ok} passing, \${bad} failing\`);
"
Command
/Users/akouta/Projects/stackra-frontend
node -e '
const fs = require("node:fs");
const path = require("node:path");
const s = JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync(".ref/schemas/catalog.v1.json","utf8"));
const props = s.properties;
const dirs = [
  "backend-packages/identity",
  "backend-packages/growth",
  "backend-packages/platform",
  "backend-packages/sdk"
];
const namePattern = new RegExp(props.name.pattern);
let ok = 0, bad = 0, seen = 0;
for (const d of dirs) {
  for (const e of fs.readdirSync(d, {withFileTypes:true})) {
    if (!e.isDirectory()) continue;
    const p = path.join(d, e.name, "catalog.json");
    if (!fs.existsSync(p)) continue;
    seen++;
    let raw;
    try { raw = JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync(p,"utf8")); }
    catch (err) { console.log("PARSE-FAIL", p, err.message); bad++; continue; }
    const issues = [];
    for (const k of s.required) if (!(k in raw)) issues.push("missing:" + k);
    for (const k of Object.keys(raw)) if (!(k in props)) issues.push("extra:" + k);
    if (!namePattern.test(raw.name)) issues.push("name-pattern:" + raw.name);
    if (!props.tier.enum.includes(raw.tier)) issues.push("tier:" + raw.tier);
    if (!props.kind.enum.includes(raw.kind)) issues.push("kind:" + raw.kind);
    for (const sur of (raw.surfaces||[])) if (!props.surfaces.items.enum.includes(sur)) issues.push("surface:" + sur);
    if (raw.purpose && raw.purpose.length > props.purpose.maxLength) issues.push("purpose-len:" + raw.purpose.length);
    for (const c of (raw.capabilities||[])) if (c.length > props.capabilities.items.maxLength) issues.push("cap-len:" + c.length);
    if (!props.maturity.enum.includes(raw.maturity)) issues.push("maturity:" + raw.maturity);
    if (issues.length) { console.log("FAIL", p, issues); bad++; }
    else ok++;
  }
}
console.log(seen + " seen, " + ok + " passing, " + bad + " failing");
'

for (const d of dirs) {
  for (const e of fs.readdirSync(d, {withFileTypes:true})) {
    if (!e.isDirectory()) continue;
    const cat = path.join(d, e.name, "catalog.json");
    const comp = path.join(d, e.name, "composer.json");
    if (!fs.existsSync(cat) || !fs.existsSync(comp)) continue;
    const catName = JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync(cat,"utf8")).name;
    const compName = JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync(comp,"utf8")).name;
    if (catName !== compName) {
      console.log("MISMATCH", d + "/" + e.name, "catalog=" + catName, "composer=" + compName);
      mismatches++;
    }
  }
}
console.log("mismatches:", mismatches);
